column1: Text

Early in the 20th century, the Model T Ford and similar cars revolutionized transportation. Today, a pressing question arises: are we overly reliant on cars? I concur that a reduction in car usage is necessary.

Elizabeth Rosenthal’s piece, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars" underscores the importance of this issue. Due to the pervasiveness of cars globally, from Shanghai to Chicago, experts note the difficulty in curtailing automotive pollution. Astonishingly, cars account for 12% of Europe's pollution; however, in the U.S., this figure skyrockets to 50% owing to extensive car usage! Moreover, Robert Duffer’s article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," throws into sharp relief the detrimental environmental impact of cars. Paris implemented driving bans to combat smog.

Conversely, Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," offers a beacon of hope. Millions of Colombians participated in a car-free day, opting for walking, cycling, or buses, resulting in unprecedented tranquility on the streets. Annually, this event prohibits private vehicles, permitting buses and taxis and significantly improving air quality. Furthermore, Bogota has undergone a remarkable transformation, with the addition of parks, recreational facilities, sidewalks, and shops.

In essence, reduced car dependence and car-free initiatives yield significant benefits, including improved air quality and enhanced urban environments. Bogota's car-free day exemplifies the potential for traffic reduction and improved city life. Adopting similar strategies in America, such as promoting cycling and walking for short trips, could pave the way for a more sustainable future. Ultimately, advocating for reduced car usage is a wise and beneficial endeavor.

Transportation is key for every country, helping people travel easily with cars and trains. Yet, lots of cars cause big problems: pollution is everywhere! Because we drive so much, the air becomes dirty. Therefore, we need to find ways to cut down on pollution fast.

Think about Vauban, a small town in Germany. Not many people there use cars. Most people live close to shops and other important things, making it easy to get around. While cars still exist, many Vauban residents don't own one. Interestingly, over half of the families gave up their cars when they moved there. They say they feel less stressed because they don't have to drive as much. Today, cars make up 12% of all pollution, but in busy places like many parts of the US, it can climb as high as 50%.

When Paris had terrible smog, it tried something new. On Mondays, cars with plates ending in even numbers could not drive. If they did, they got big fines. The next day, cars with odd numbers faced the same rule. In just five days, traffic dropped by 60%. Diesel cars, common in France, made the smog worse. Still, the ban only worked for a short time because they stopped doing it every other day.

Imagine a day without cars, like in Bogota, Colombia. Once a year, they only allow taxis and buses. This one day really lowers pollution, and they’ve been doing it since the 1990s. Later, they made bike paths, parks, and places to have fun. It may be hard, but not using cars helps the earth.

While we probably won’t get rid of cars completely anytime soon, countries should try to pollute less and encourage less driving. Doing things to fight air pollution will make things better. Even though we use cars a lot, driving too much hurts the world and us.

So, Elisabeth Rosenthal wonders, are Americans leaving their cars behind? Her main point? Driving less is becoming a thing, which is potentially great! Think: fewer crashes, healthier folks, and cleaner air from less driving. Maybe we just don't need cars like before.

First, less driving seriously boosts our health and safety – super important, right? Andrew Selsky recalled how relaxed the streets in Colombia were on a car-free day. Less cars, less stress! Jams ruin plans and make us mad. So, less driving means less traffic and less stress! Heidrun Walter felt happier and less stressed after selling her car. Plus, more exercise. Sure, cars are easy, but we forget to move. Selsky saw tons of new parks and sports spots. Less cars could mean more parks, making us want to exercise. Basically, less driving equals less stress and a healthier life!

Also, less driving is good for the planet. Maybe not everyone cares, but it's a big deal. Pollution hurts everything, and cars pollute a lot. Rosenthal says less driving "will have beneficial implications for carbon emissions and the environment." Less driving, less pollution – a better future chance. Pollution is a huge issue, and drivers worsen it.

Plus, we might not need cars as much. Many think they MUST have them, but are they addicted? Tech has changed us, and we're forgetting nature. Too much dependence isn't good. David Goldberg says "all our development since World War II has been centered on the car, and that will have to change." Some might disagree, but depending on something bad isn't progress. Rosenthal notes "cash-strapped Americans could not afford new cars, and the unemployed weren't going to work anyway." Not everyone can even afford a car. Car-dependent people have forgotten nature and don’t always think about the environment. Less driving could fix that.

In short, less driving could mean we're healthier, there's less pollution, and cars aren't as needed. It's a cleaner, safer world. Maybe America is improving by driving less and thinking about what’s good for all.

Do you ever just drive and drive? Maybe it's to work or just to the store? It's crazy how much everyone drives, even for little trips. It's easy to forget that all this driving hurts the planet. Maybe don't drive today? People always say, "But I need my car!" But you can drive less. Cities like Havana, Paris, and Bogotá show it can be done. Really, less driving is good for you and the Earth.

Believe it or not, less driving might make you happier! Heidrun Walter said that people are way less stressed when they don't drive. Think about how angry you get in traffic. Driving stinks – finding your way, dealing with mean drivers – it all makes you stressed. Big cities like New York and Paris are known for awful traffic and dirty air. Robert Duffer, in his article "Paris bans driving due to smog," wrote about how warm air traps car fumes, which is terrible. Duffer also talked about how Paris temporarily banned cars because of bad pollution, and the air got better fast! Instead, people could walk, bike, or even skate. It's healthier and makes you happier than sitting in traffic.

If we drive less, cities would look way better. Pollution hurts plants and smells gross, making everything sad. Less driving means fewer bad fumes and cleaner cities. Elisabeth Rosenthal, in "In German Suburb, Life goes on Without Cars," said that fewer cars let more people walk around, which leads to more sidewalks and cool stores. Andrew Selsky mentioned new parks, sports areas, nice sidewalks, restaurants, and stores. The EPA likes areas with fewer cars, as Rosenthal said, because it really cuts down on pollution. Bogotá, Colombia, even has a "no car" day and fines drivers who break the rules. Fines are annoying, but they work to reduce smog! Basically: less driving is great for you, makes cities better, and helps clean up the planet.

Automobiles? People adore 'em, sure, but they are REALLY vital. We rely on them for transport! Public transit is available, of course, but many prefer a private ride. However, excessive auto traffic sparks contamination and anxiety. Switching to buses or trains results in reduced repair expenses, less road congestion, and purified air. As such, choosing to drive less is astute.

Using automobiles less benefits humanity and our environment, primarily through improved air quality. Internal combustion engines eject noxious emissions. A single vehicle? A minor issue. However, vast quantities, as found in Paris, create substantial concerns. Paris enforced "car bans!" as a consequence of air pollution. As highlighted by Robert Duffer, cars with even-numbered license plates faced temporary driving prohibition and subsequent penalties, followed by odd-numbered plates. This resulted in a 60% decrease in traffic! Andrew Selsky noted that Bogota, Colombia, implements vehicle-free days with the goal of pollution reduction, promotion of alternative transportation options, and environmental protection. Globally, limiting vehicle usage is viewed as a positive step!

Furthermore, think of stress reduction! Traffic-related worries, vehicle maintenance, and loud noises induce stress. Less driving equates to diminished stress. The absence of congestion, repair costs, and excessive noise contributes to a more comfortable existence. According to Elisabeth Rosenthal, Hartmut Walter stated, "I always felt tense when driving. Now I feel better.” This validates the stress-relieving potential of going car-free. Further, Andrew Selsky argues that car-free days "provide an excellent chance to relieve stress..." Prioritizing mental wellbeing is vital because stress reduction enhances overall quality of life.

Ultimately, curtailing reliance on automobiles is advantageous for both individuals and the world. Our Earth needs help, and stress is debilitating. Minimizing car use will help the environment and decrease stress. Granted, adapting can be challenging, but multiple locations are adopting these steps, and they're effective! It fosters a healthier, well-rounded world.

Isn't the Electoral College just plain weird? Forget voting directly for the top job; instead, we handpick people to do it, whether they lean left or right. But wait a minute! Source two tells us these electors can actually ignore our choices! Is that fair? We cast our ballots hoping for positive changes, but this system complicates things. And the worst part? It's that winner-takes-all game!

We don't directly choose the president. Source two says we pick electors. Each state gets a certain number. These electors then pick the winner. Number two mentions there are 538 electors; 270 is the magic number. A state's elector count is linked to its representation in Congress. To make things truly fair, every single vote should matter! Getting rid of the Electoral College guarantees every vote counts. Candidates should have to earn votes with genuine support, and ties? Practically nonexistent. As it stands now, if the majority in your state want someone else, your vote is basically useless. Ditch the college, and BAM, your vote matters!

Source two raises the issue of "faithless" electors, which means some might switch sides and vote for the opposition! Talk about a betrayal! A Republican elector voting Democrat cancels out all Republican votes. We elect a president every four years. Electors changing their minds? Absolutely not!

Sources two and three emphasize the "winner-take-all" rule. It's a tough break. If you lose, your votes are worthless. The winner gets everything! It's almost as if the loser's voters secretly wanted the winner all along.

Senators, dump the Electoral College! It's a flawed system. It favors some votes over others. It allows electors to do whatever they please. And that "winner-take-all" rule? It throws away votes for those who didn't win in the trash.

Dear Senator,

I'm writing about a really important change: how we pick our President. Let's think about just getting rid of the Electoral College. We really need a system that represents everyone better, like, now! Right now, this old system isn't fair; electors can go against what the people want – that's just not right!

I know changing the Electoral College might be hard. But, it would be worth it in the long run. If every single vote counted directly, candidates would really try to win people over. We also have to think about what happens if there's a tie and how to make sure things stay fair. So, even if totally getting rid of the Electoral College isn't the best plan, fixing its problems is something we have to do.

Right now, electors have way too much power in deciding who wins. The "winner-takes-all" thing with votes in the Electoral College makes people feel left out and like their votes don't matter. Candidates spend most of their time and money in just a few "swing" states, and forget about the rest. Like in 2000, 17 states were totally ignored. Obviously, because of stuff like this, the Electoral College is old and needs to be fixed or ditched.

Candidates carefully pick electors to help them win. These electors help them get support without the candidate having to go to every state. Basically, the candidate just waits to see what happens. A candidate could win without even trying that hard because the electors decide everything. This makes it possible for candidates to maybe even bribe electors in important states – that would be bad.

When people vote, they're not really voting for the candidates themselves. They're actually voting for electors, which is kind of strange. Electors are chosen in different ways, like at state meetings or by the candidates themselves. A big problem with the Electoral College is that something unexpected could happen. For example, in 1960, some people in Louisiana thought about replacing the Democratic electors with people who didn't like John F. Kennedy.

Basically, the Electoral College is an old thing from a long time ago. It gives electors too much power, which isn't fair to the voters. It might have made sense back then, but it doesn't fit with how democracy should work today.

Okay, everyone, listen closely! We're surrounded by cars, right? They seem easy to use. But what if we drove less? Think about it: cleaner air, and more money in your pocket. Seriously, less driving? It's a great idea.

Take Paris, for example. Robert Duffer wrote, "Paris bans driving due to smog." Their idea was pretty clever. One day, cars with odd-numbered plates could drive; the next day, only even numbers. Break the rules, get a fine! They even made public transport free for a week! Traffic and smog? Gone! The air got better, and people saved money. Duffer said traffic dropped a lot – like 60%! He said, "Traffic jams dropped 60 percent in Paris, after five days of smog so thick… it was almost as bad as Beijing, China, a city known for pollution." Even with bad smog, fewer cars made a HUGE difference.

Now, look at Vauban, Germany. Elisabeth Rosenthal reported, "In German Suburb, life goes on without cars." They went all out, getting rid of cars entirely. About 70% don’t even own a car because parking is super expensive outside the town, and cars are usually not allowed anyway. And they love it there. Heidrun Walter said, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Many young people don’t even bother getting a license! Rosenthal wrote in "The end of car culture" that cars are just a way to get around. No one cares much about the car itself, just getting around easily.

The point is, cars are useful, but so are buses, trains, bikes, and carpools. And guess what? Some of these are even free! Also, less pollution and traffic? That’s a big win for our health and the planet's health, too.

Plumer just thinks the Electoral College is wrong. Source 2 says it's old and doesn't make sense. Lots of people agree; they think it's a disaster or just bad. Some say it stops ties or helps big states, but many don't buy it. They want a simple popular vote because it's better.

The big problem is votes aren't equal. Electors have way more power than regular folks. If we voted directly, everyone's vote would count the same. Plus, electors can be bribed or influenced, which is bad for democracy.

A lot of people think the Electoral College is old news. Plumer (Source 2) even quotes Bob Dole saying, "Get rid of the Electoral College!" Lots of folks feel that way. They want to vote directly and skip the middleman. Some joke that a kid with darts could pick a better president. Plumer (Source 2) says, "Over 60% of voters want a direct election," and even more do since 2000.

Honestly, the Electoral College is weird. It's a complicated system where electors, not us, pick the president. Plumer (Source 2) says, "You vote for electors, then they vote for the president. Who are these electors? Just normal people. Who picks them? Depends on the state. Can we control them? Not always! Do people mess up? Sometimes!" Makes you wonder who's really in charge.

Sure, the Electoral College might have some good points, like keeping things steady, but most think it's not worth it. A simple vote by the people seems fairer. Like Plumer (Source 2) says, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational!"

From the get-go in the United States, the Electoral College found its spot in the Constitution. I reckon we should keep it 'cause, so far, it's been working fine, so why fix what ain't broke? Sticking with the Electoral College is a smart move; here's why, in a nutshell, with five quick points.

Alright, so the Electoral College ain't all sunshine and rainbows. For example, electors *could* go off-script and vote as they please. We're not exactly voting for the prez; we're voting for these electors, and they're the ones who pick the president. They could totally ignore the people and do their own thing. Also, a state might send in two different lists of electors, which would be a total mess. And even though it's rare, the Electoral College could end up in a tie. 'Cause of the "winner-takes-all" rule, candidates sometimes skip some states 'cause one party always wins there. With a straight-up vote, they might try harder everywhere to get more votes. Still, gripes are usually about unlikely stuff; because of this, it's better to chill out and consider another outlook.

Swing states are the big deal for who wins, so their voters are usually more dialed in. 'Cause they know their votes matter, they really pay attention to the candidates and the news. Candidates spend a ton of time and dough trying to win them over. Lots of other states are basically decided before anyone votes, 'cause one party is super popular. Maybe voters in swing states think more about who they actually like and what they stand for; maybe they're the best folks to elect our president.

The Electoral College helps us dodge needing extra votes, which keeps things less confusing. Without it, a really close direct vote could stir up a lot of trouble. The Electoral College's usefulness is pretty clear when you check out Nixon's win in '68 and Clinton's in '92; both won with around 43% of the votes.

I see why some voters in states that always vote one way might not dig the Electoral College. Since their votes might not seem to matter, they might not even bother to vote at all. One person's vote doesn't mean much on its own, and back in 2012, some folks just didn't vote. For some, just saying who they like is more crucial than actually changing the election. If you're rolling in government, your vote is way more likely to shake things up.

Esteemed Senator,

Hear me out, we simply cannot abolish the Electoral College! This system involves 538 individuals. The key to winning lies in securing upwards of 270 votes. The states appoint electors, commonly linked to a particular candidate. It is of paramount importance that we preserve this system. The reason? It ensures clarity in declaring the winner, period.

But why maintain it, you ask? Truthfully, the Electoral College almost invariably gives us a definitive victor, devoid of unnecessary complications. Could there be a tie? Hypothetically, but the odds are slim, even with the current arrangement of electors we have. Take 2012, when Obama clinched approximately 62% of electoral votes, despite securing only 51% of the popular vote. Most states operate under a winner-take-all structure. And so, a narrow victory within a state can translate into winning a substantial number of electoral votes. But then again, this compels candidates to heavily target swing states, potentially neglecting others. Nonetheless, the ultimate victor often amasses a significantly higher proportion of electoral votes than popular votes.

Furthermore, the President should be a figure for *everyone*. Thus, the Electoral College compels candidates to court votes from every corner of the nation. No individual area possesses a sufficient concentration of votes to unilaterally elect the president. Irrespective of extreme popularity in one locality, extensive campaigning throughout the country is still required. Amassing additional votes in regions where popularity already exists does not yield any additional electoral votes. A president lacking widespread support? Now, that's a recipe for disaster. It would breed a sense of disenfranchisement among the people.

Ultimately, upholding the Electoral College is a *necessity*. It effectively mitigates the risk of ties and guarantees that the president is diligently serving *all* citizens.

Heidrun Walter once hated cars, but not anymore! She feels totally free. This is happening everywhere, with folks ditching their cars. We thought cars were a must, but now sales are slipping, and fewer people are driving. They're seeing that less car use is a good thing. It can really help stop climate change, end pollution, and make the economy better for everyone, not just car companies.

Car fumes worsen the greenhouse effect, which harms us and nature. "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars" says cars cause 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases – almost half in some US cities! Less driving means less pollution, which is great for our planet. That can help fight big problems like global warming and rising seas. So, fewer cars mean fewer greenhouse gases!

Smog and pollution are gross. They hurt our health and our world. The article, "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog", says, "After Days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city." The air got much better in just five days! More of this could reduce pollution a lot. It might even get people to try buses, bikes, or walking! "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" shows that banning cars can lead to new bike paths, parks, sports centers, better sidewalks, and less traffic. Basically, less driving makes city life better!

Finally, less driving helps the economy in lots of ways. One source said that in the US, we spend so much money on highways. If we didn't need cars as much, that money could go to schools, programs, and local stuff. Also, less driving saves people money, so they can spend it on other things. One source said that "Demographic shifts in the driving population suggest that the trend may accelerate. There has been a large drop in the percentage of 16 to 39yearolds getting a license." This lets us focus on making the economy better. So, less driving means more government money, more savings for people, and growth for everyone.

To sum up, less car use is really good. It helps the economy, stops the greenhouse effect, and ends pollution in cities.

So, picking a president? Huge! Their choices? Big impact, good or bad. The Electoral College? Supposed to show what each state wants, but does it really work? I think it does a pretty good job of showing where each state stands and giving us a feel for what the whole country is thinking.

Some people bash the Electoral College. They say it doesn't always pick the best person or that it's just not fair. But hey, we get to CHOOSE! What we think matters, and every single vote really counts – way more than not voting at all. It's not perfect, but what is? Overall, I think the Electoral College does a decent job of counting up the votes.

Figuring out a better way is tough. A main worry is that the Electoral College might not really show who most people voted for. But I don't buy that. Our votes help steer the Electoral College towards the person we want, and I like how it gets states working together by showing what each state mostly wants.

Okay, some say bigger states have too much power in the Electoral College. Maybe a little, but it kind of makes sense, right? States with more people should get more votes. Still, each vote counts the same, so the big guys can't totally boss everyone around. Even when we don't agree, the Electoral College gives us a fair way to see what the country is thinking, which is cool.

Basically, the Electoral College figures out what each state wants by using the most votes to pick a winner. It's not perfect, but just adding up all the votes might be worse. Every system has problems, but the Electoral College gives us a pretty organized way to count votes over time. But hey, you can't please everyone!

Dear Senator, I’ve thought a lot about the best way to pick our President and their team, and I’ve got an idea. Basically, there are two options: the Electoral College, or counting all the votes from each person. After thinking about it, one seems way better.

With the Electoral College, certain people called electors vote for the President and Vice President. These 538 people decide who wins. To be honest, I don't like this system. We think we're directly electing the president, but really, we're just choosing electors who then make the decision. Why should a small group, compared to millions of voters, have so much power in choosing our leader, leaving us waiting to see what they decide? It feels unfair that their opinions matter so much more than regular people when it comes to who runs the country.

On the other hand, a popular vote system lets each person directly vote for who they want as president and their team. That way, we can clearly say who we want to lead us, hoping for good changes. Even if a candidate loses, your vote still matters because it adds to the national voice. The Electoral College might be faster, but knowing your vote really counts could get more people involved and interested in the candidates and what they stand for. We get to shape our future, instead of just hoping the electors agree.

Okay, the Electoral College has some good points, like making sure there’s a clear winner. Some might say that choosing electors is the same as directly voting. But these small benefits don't compare to giving every citizen the right to vote directly, which is a basic part of democracy.

In the end, when thinking about how to pick the president, the main question is: How do you want your voice to be heard? Do you want to be directly involved, or are you okay with letting others decide? For me, the answer is clear: I support the system that makes our democracy stronger.

Think of a nice family walk in the park, but ugh, the yucky pollution spoils it! It's super obvious we use our cars way too much. Maybe try taking the bus sometimes, or walking, or even sharing rides? Seriously, driving less really helps! Cars are so pricey, they get in the way of other ways to travel, and they're bad for the planet.

Having a car can cost you more than you think! Like, in Vauban, Germany, a parking spot could be a whopping $40,000 - that's more than some houses cost! And if you drive on the wrong day in Bogota, Columbia, you'll get a $25 ticket. Driving in Paris? Could cost you 22 euros just because of your license plate! And gas? Always expensive! Rich folks might not care, but families with kids sure do!

It's easy to forget how great public transport is. Imagine if there were no buses, trains, or subways. Try driving into New York City then, good luck! Bogota makes people want to take the bus or walk by fining drivers on days when you can't use your car. The mayor, Antanas Mockus, was super happy with how many people joined in, even when it rained! Other cities like Cali and Valledupar did the same, and a lot of people were interested. Even New York lets you borrow bikes. No matter what, public transport is key to keeping the environment safe.

Yikes, sometimes the smog is so thick you can't even see the stars. Paris stopped cars from driving for a bit because there was too much pollution, and the air got 60% cleaner in just five days! But, France likes using diesel fuel, which is really bad for pollution, so that makes things tricky. Bogota's car-free day is a nice break from the smog, and Vauban doesn't have much smog because people don't drive much there! Even small changes can make a big difference for the Earth.

Vauban and Bogota show us that less driving means less pollution. Paris bans cars to fight smog. Vauban makes parking super expensive so people don't drive. Bogota fines drivers to get them to walk or take the bus. We've got to stop using our cars so much because it saves money, helps us find other ways to get around, and makes the air cleaner.

Less driving does our town good! Places like France and Colombia are thinking of different ways for us to get around. It's more chill when we don't always have to drive. Research even says fewer folks here own cars. It's the perfect time to try out cool ideas, just like in Vauban or Bogota, so we aren't stuck in traffic all day.

Here's a thought: a town in Germany shows us it's possible to live without cars. Elisabeth Rosenthal said Vauban, Germany, is pretty much "car-free." Most people there dig using public transport. The article also said "70% of families don't have cars, and lots sell theirs when they move there!" One person said they feel calmer and happier without a car because they don't have the headache of driving. Many spots want to be like this, cutting down on cars to help the planet. Expect more "smart planning" down the road. If we try to drive less, our town could become a car-free dream.

Bogota, Colombia, has car-free days so people can test out other ways to travel and clean up the air. Visitors always say the air feels so much better. Going "car-free" helps people get more active and makes the city look great. Reports show more parks and sports fields, wide, clean sidewalks, and cool shops and restaurants. It's not only about cleaner air; it makes the city a nicer and more beautiful place to hang out!

Picture using ideas like Bogota and Vauban do. Fewer people around here are already buying cars. Some are even ditching driver's ed. One writer noticed Americans aren't as crazy about cars these days. Another said we drove the most miles a while ago, and it's been going down ever since. Young folks are a big part of this change. They use social media, phones, carpools, and buses more often, so they don't drive as much. Car companies might not be thrilled, but lots of us think we'll have better and easier ways to travel in the future because of it.

Bottom line, less driving means less pollution and stress, nicer neighborhoods, more exercise, and saving resources. Cities everywhere are trying to reduce cars to help the environment. If we go green and car-free, we'll be part of a change that's happening all over the world!

Dear Senator, I really want you to get rid of the Electoral College. I feel strongly that who becomes president should only depend on who wins the most votes across the whole country. To be honest, the Electoral College seems really unfair and like it could really hurt what the U.S. Constitution stands for.

The Electoral College messes up the basic ideas of what makes our country a democracy. We vote, sure, but the Electoral College can change what everyone wants, and that's just not fair! It makes you wonder what our democracy is really like. Think about the 2000 election: Gore got more votes, but Bush won because of the Electoral College. Like "The Indefensible Electoral College" says, "More than 60% of voters want a simple election, not this confusing mess." It's obvious people want an easy way to pick their leader.

Also, I really don't like the Electoral College because it could weaken the U.S. Constitution, especially our important rights. The main idea is that we, the people, get to pick our president. But when the person with the most votes loses because of the Electoral College, that hurts democracy. "The Indefensible Electoral College" worries about possible problems with elections, asking: "What if a state sends in two different groups of electors? This happened in Hawaii in '60. Luckily, Nixon just agreed to the electors picked for his opponent." Just imagine the chaos if that happened again and someone won who shouldn't! The Electoral College is like a disaster waiting to happen.

So, let's get rid of the Electoral College! It's just not fair and could really damage our U.S. Constitution. I really hope you think about this when you're making decisions about this important issue.

It's a widespread worry how transportation affects air quality. People might not fully grasp the good things that could happen for the environment if we drove less. Cars are always pumping out pollution. So, really think hard before you buy a car, because what it costs the earth might be way more than how easy it makes your life.

Think about all the cars, buses, and huge trucks out there. All the fuel they burn really messes up the environment. The stuff that's left over from burning fuel makes the planet worse. If things keep going the way they are, we could run out of ozone, and then Earth would be a terrible place to live! Plus, lots of folks drive way too much, even when they could walk or bike instead. When Paris made rules to stop people from driving when the air was bad, traffic went down by a huge 60% in just five days. The way we get around can be really bad for the environment.

Having a car can cost you way more than you think. In Vauban, Germany, if you want to own a car, you have to buy a parking spot that costs almost $40,000, and that's not even counting how much the house costs! Gas is super expensive. Also, car insurance adds even more to what you have to pay. On top of all that, driving can be scary, stressful, and not fun at all.

Oh, cars! They're everywhere, right? Seriously, they're a huge part of our lives. But what if we got rid of them? That'd be a HUGE win for the Earth! Too many cars equal awful pollution and just ick, unhealthy air.

Bogota, Colombia tried out a "No Car Day"—a super cool idea that other cities should try! People walked, biked, or hopped on the bus instead of driving, and BAM! The city cleared right up! Even with a little rain, who cares? Most people still went along with it. The mayor said the weather didn't matter that much. The aim? Cleaner air by using other ways to travel. But doing this in the U.S.? That might be tough because we LOVE our cars!

Paris fought car smog by stopping people from driving when the air got super gross. At first, folks weren't happy campers. Reuters said they gave out almost 4,000 tickets and towed 27 cars! But traffic dropped like crazy – by 60%! Still, the air was still kinda smoggy, just like in Beijing. It's up in the air if people will stick with these rules forever.

Driving less might bug some people because getting around could be harder! BUT, new studies say fewer Americans are buying cars, driving them, or even getting their licenses. A professor at Drexel University thinks things are shifting. People stopped driving 'cause they HAD to, so maybe they won't go back. Car use in the U.S. might stay low, which could make the air better.

Cars are trashing the environment everywhere. Driving less is hard, and it has its downsides. But the good stuff is clear as day! Just imagine cleaner air and more people walking, biking, and skating. Changing how we use cars? That'll REALLY help the environment. Less driving? It's like saving the planet!

Seriously slashing how much we drive? It all boils down to *where* you are and, like, how much you *really* want it. All over the planet, some towns are messing around with the idea of fewer cars, or even NO cars. You know, places drowning in smog are usually super motivated to get cars off the road, just so everyone can breathe easier.

Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb...", paints a pretty picture of what it's like to live without a car. When you've got the right setup – bam! – walking and biking suddenly become way more appealing. And *that's* a huge deal. I mean, when you think about all the pollution, giving this a shot somewhere else? It's definitely something to think about.

Some cities are just choking on pollution, it's awful. In Duffer's "Paris bans driving...", he talks about how Paris' air got so nasty, they actually banned driving! People got ticked off, of course, so if you broke the rules, you got slapped with a fine and they might even take your car away! Duffer points out that "Congestion was down 60 percent..." – which proves that the ban seriously worked to cut down traffic.

All over the world, there are plans to get people moving without polluting. Selsky's "Carfree day..." shines a light on how awesome Bogota did. Seriously, millions ditched their cars and walked, biked, or skated – even when it was pouring rain! Selsky mentions that Bogota just feels *nicer* now. They spruced up the "Parks…sidewalks" making the city better for everyone.

Things are changing, and fast! Young people are like, "Do I *really* need a car?" They're leaning towards stuff that's cheaper and better for the planet. The whole idea of *needing* a car? It's slowly fading away, but it's still hanging around.

The Electoral College: A Seriously contentious subject! Should it be abolished or maintained? Isn't the setup a bit bizarre? Citizens are essentially voting for individuals who, in turn, select the president. If the United States champions popular sovereignty, shouldn't the candidate with the most votes simply prevail?

When casting a ballot for president, what action are citizens *actually* undertaking? Are voters merely choosing someone to cast a vote later? Is that the only purpose? The article "Does the Electoral College Work?" argues voters are selecting "your candidate's electors." However, if the Constitution stipulates that the people directly vote for president, then how does this system translate in reality? The process seems contrary to the very essence of American principles. Are those electors truly trustworthy to align with the voters will? "The Indefensible Electoral College" puts it, "...Can voters actually control who their electors vote for? Not always." Could your chosen candidate lose the state's vote despite being the popular choice? Is the Electoral College undermining the impact and value of direct votes, violating the democratic right to have one's voice matter? Overall, the process is convoluted and your individual voice should be heard.

Proponents assert that the Electoral College guarantees a president is elected, but in reality, does this guarantee the popular vote wins? The Electoral College seldom aligns with the popular vote, often making individual voices feel futile. The current system feels as though a fundamental American right is being taken away. The popular vote should be valued over the electoral votes. Just look at the 2000 Election! Al Gore lost the election even though he won the popular vote. "The Indefensible Electoral College" points out, "...according to a Gallup poll in 2000, taken shortly after Al Gore—thanks to the quirks of the electoral college—won the popular vote but lost the presidency..." This perfectly highlights how it undermines the importance of the individual vote. The same article points out "...over 60 percent of voters would rather vote directly than have this system." If our votes *truly* matter, then a vote should be held to determine whether to keep the Electoral College in place.

The claim that the Electoral College ensures efficiency and preempts voting irregularities should be examined for accuracy. Every system has challenges, but does the Electoral College provide a viable solution? America was established on the principle of adhering to the will of the people expressed through their votes. Direct voting, without intermediaries, should be the standard practice in America.

In essence, the Electoral College is demonstrably unfair, structurally flawed, and in conflict with core American principles. It obstructs the ability to directly elect one's preferred candidate, compelling voters to choose electors instead. Those who cherish American ideals should recognize the fundamental wrongness of this system. It’s a very ineffective approach to elections, and real American control should be implemented!

Esteemed Senator,

Why this Electoral College system, still? Some claim it harms democracy because, frankly, we vote for electors instead of our actual preferred leader. However, *it does* work! It swiftly helps select a winner and provides us with a president who represents all.

It is designed to secure a clear win. Just voting for the most popular person could create problems! Richard A. Posner highlights that the winner usually receives considerably more Electoral College votes than regular votes. This decreases the chances of disputes over who actually won. He’s correct; those votes do simplify matters. Furthermore, it prevents ties, such as if Nixon and Clinton had tied in '92. Because it considers more votes, the Electoral College distinctly determines the winner.

Additionally, it forces the president to consider everyone. Candidates require votes nationwide, not only in their popular areas. We need a president for all! Imagine Republicans solely focusing on the South. If a Republican does that, it is unfair to others. Bradford Plumer notes that candidates often ignore states they expect to lose. Yet, perhaps those states are already decided! Why would a Democrat campaign in Texas, a Republican stronghold? Or a Republican in California, a Democratic state? Voters there have probably already decided! Old opinions like Plumer's might be outdated. Situations evolve! If you dislike the Electoral College, seek updated info. Examine recent elections! Public sentiment in 2004, 2008, and 2012 hinged on events, promises, and youth turnout. Thus, the Electoral College guarantees the president cares for the entire nation.

Therefore, maintain the Electoral College! It identifies the winner and enables the president to represent all of us. Electoral College vote totals are often higher, making victory determination easy. Moreover, it compels the president to consider the whole nation, not just select regions. Senator, please, retain it! It has served us well lately and should continue.

Back then, folks just walked everywhere. Groceries, grub, and grandma's house all meant hoofing it. So, how did we get so car-crazy? Lots of cities now want fewer cars around.

Cars are bad news for the Earth. But hey, things are looking up! New laws are popping up to cut down on car use. Take Vauban, Germany—people ditch cars to help the planet, and they dig it! Heidrun Walter, a Vauban mom, said, "Cars gave me headaches. I'm way happier now!"

Paris, you know, that famous city, banned cars because the air was awful. Traffic dropped like a rock—down 60 percent—in just five days!

Paris had the grossest air in Europe, so they had to do something drastic. The car ban was a hit, cleaning up the air and wiping out smog.

Big cities like New York have streets jammed with cars. Buildings are squished together, so walking or biking often beats driving. New York's got cool bike lanes. Plus, carpooling is a hit, with people sharing rides to use fewer cars. That's less pollution, fewer traffic jams, and saving cash on gas.

Bill Ford, the big cheese at Ford, dreams of car ownership becoming a pain. He wants to team up with phone companies to build cities where "people walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses all play nice, saving time and energy, cutting pollution, and boosting safety." Cars are handy, so we can't just ban them everywhere. But using them less is a win. Too many cars mean too much pollution! How about a car-free day to try out other ways to get around? Biking rocks for exercise and is faster than walking, so you get fit and arrive sooner!

Alright, presidential elections, right? It's a rough ride with the Electoral College. Should we keep it, or get rid of it? It mixes votes from normal people and Congress. Past elections? They were a mess. The future? Might be even crazier. Shouldn't we just directly vote for the president in a democracy? Seriously, we need a fix and should dump the Electoral College now.

Here's the weird part: We don't pick the president directly; we pick electors who do it. And even when the popular vote wins, "winner-take-all" messes things up. "The Indefensible Electoral College" shows how this hurts voters. Candidates pick favorites, ignoring states they can't win and focusing on swing states. Some states get attention; others don't. In 2000, seventeen states had zero visits. How messed up is that?

And remember "the disaster factor." Bradford Plumer thinks we got lucky in 2000, but the system could crash hard. Imagine Louisiana almost swapping out Democratic electors to mess with Kennedy. Things could get that wild! Or, states could send different sets of electors, or we could tie. Even with small changes, we need a much better system.

Richard A. Posner defends the Electoral College, saying it keeps things stable. He says it helps smaller states compete with bigger ones. But that's not balanced; it favors smaller states. Posner also thinks voters in swing states get more attention. Plumer disagrees, noting campaigns ignored places like Rhode Island and South Carolina. Many voters in big TV areas saw no ads. The bottom line: the Electoral College is easy to manipulate and must go.

I still say: "Get rid of the Electoral College." People should directly choose their president. It's hard to fix, but the current system is clearly unfair. Some defend the Electoral College, but seriously! We need a total overhaul or at least major changes to this thing.

Dear Florida State Senator, The Electoral College? It's sparking fights! People praise it, saying it picks presidents well enough. Others? They shout it's not fair and needs a big fix. We need updates to deal with the problems we have. Shouldn't our system show what we *really* want in a real democracy?

Supporters say Electoral College ties hardly ever happen; electors usually vote how they should. But who cares if issues aren't common? It doesn't make it okay! Tiny cracks turn huge, like holes in socks. Patch it up now, or get ready for a bigger mess! Electors mostly listen, but might just go rogue!

Does the Electoral College steal your vote's thunder? You vote, sure, but electors have final say. States get tons of electors, which just feels icky. Some votes count way more than others! Folks skip voting, thinking it's all pointless! Candidates only care about swing states. Voters feel lost.

Besides people not caring, the Electoral College has big flaws. Ties? Rare, yeah, but they can happen. Then, the House picks, maybe against what everyone else wants. And you could win the whole shebang but still lose the election—remember Al Gore back in 2000? Told ya! It’s busted. Fix it *before* even bigger chaos.

The Electoral College kinda works, but it doesn't show what people *really* want. Electors pick the president, not us regular folks. That's a problem when we say we're a government by the people. There's got to be a better way to make democracy. Speak up! Ditch the Electoral College and push for a system that mirrors what America *really* stands for.

Alright, let’s see if this keeps us safe. Here we go!

Cars get us places fast, sure, but all that pollution hurts the Earth, big time. Driving less can help fix this mess, and here’s why: less greenhouse gas, less smog, and resources saved!

For starters, driving less drops greenhouse gases like a rock. Bike it! Train it! Walk to work, even! Cars cough out exhaust, which turns into nasty gases that mess with the planet. One study (we'll call it Source 1) says regular cars make about HALF of these gases! Fewer cars equals a happier Earth, period.

Plus, ditching the car slashes smog dramatically. Smog’s just awful stuff, bad for the environment and you! Think of smog as super-gross air that makes you feel sick. Lots of cars can make smog REALLY bad in some places. Source 2 showed Paris's air was once almost as bad as China’s, and everyone knows China's air is terrible. Driving less means cleaner air, plain and simple! Paris cut smog by 60% in just five days when people drove less! Clean air is a huge win!

Finally, driving less saves resources! Source 1 says that if we don’t rely on cars so much, cities won’t need to get so big. Less land needed for roads, shops, and houses! Source 4 says this saves tons of building stuff and protects nature's treasures. Less driving makes life better for everyone.

Basically, less driving is good for the planet, good for you, and saves stuff for the future.

Alright, so a bunch of people really dislike the Electoral College. But wait a minute! It could have some good points. The Constitution actually set it up this way on purpose. The idea? Keep either Congress or the people from having too much power over who becomes president. Think of it as a safety net. It makes sure everyone in the country gets a say, truly. State leaders get a real chance to champion their people's desires. Sure, there's some griping, but it's still a decent way to pick the President, isn't it?

Presidential candidates? The Electoral College pushes them to hustle! They can't just focus on one area, like only the West, and assume victory. No way! They need to win over folks who may initially disagree with them. This is great! A President with wide appeal will likely do a much better job for everyone. Also, all that attention on swing states? It excites people, informs them, and prepares them to make a smart decision about who to elect. Seriously!

And really, sticking with the Electoral College makes sense. Why? It usually picks the same person the popular vote picks! Okay, back in 2000, Gore got more votes overall, but Bush won the presidency because of the Electoral College. Crazy, right? But, it hardly ever happens! Before that, you'd have to go back to 1888! The Electoral College voters? Parties choose them, and they almost always vote for their party's candidate, like they should. Ditch the Electoral College and just use the popular vote? Could cause new problems. What about a tie? Oh no! Super unlikely, yes, but what if? The Electoral College helps when no one gets a clear majority.

Some people argue the Electoral College makes folks not bother voting in states where their candidate has little chance, like Democrats in Texas. But every vote is meaningful. It still impacts how a state's electoral votes are allocated. People vote in presidential elections to show support, not only to cast the deciding vote. The Electoral College has worked for over a century, and it will likely continue to do well.

Want to save Earth? Drive less! A study in Europe says that cars make up around 12% of greenhouse gas emissions. Giving up your car may be tough. But, there are many options! How about carpooling, getting a ride, hopping on a bus or train, grabbing a taxi, or biking? In the end, saving the planet requires that you act!

Cars harm our air! Some cities make you pay to drive less. Paris tried it out. They stopped cars for a bit after bad smog! This shows that change can occur! Beijing, once as dirty as Paris, was cleaned up by its government. Bogota stops 7.2 million people from driving on "Day Without Cars." If you do it wrong, you pay $25 or go to jail. "This plan might spread," said Andrew Selsky, who said that millions of Colombians "walked, biked, skated, or took buses." The air is cleaner, and folks hang out in parks.

Driving less helps you a lot! Less traffic means cleaner air. Walking more cuts issues with your heart, obesity, etc.! Car companies may not like this. Ford, Toyota, Honda, etc., might lose money if fewer cars sell. But people will still drive. However, less car making could help the economy by cutting gas costs. Guess what? Driving is going down each year! Experts are happy for Earth, even if car companies get worried.

See? Use cars less! Young people don't like cars. One study in 2013 said that youth driving fell by 23% from 2001-2009. Experts are happy, but the sales drop makes us ask if the U.S. is at "peak driving." What will happen now? Good or bad? Who knows! Obama’s plans to cut greenhouse gases seem to work. Studies say "Americans buy fewer cars, drive less and get fewer licenses each year."

So, Senator, regarding this Electoral College, shouldn't we maybe opt for the candidate with the most individual votes? Straight up popular vote. A lot of people don't even bother voting—like, almost half—because they think it's pointless, especially in states where the result is basically decided beforehand, per "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23. Politicians only seem to care about a few key states.

"In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23 points out, "only half of eligible Americans voted in 2012," Senator. Why bother if your candidate has zero chance in your state? Plus, people have historically tried to manipulate the Electoral College. For instance, segregationists schemed to undermine Democratic Electors to undermine Kennedy, which "The Indefensible Electoral College" Section 11 highlights.

But some argue for keeping it. They say it's a compromise made by the Founding Fathers for fairness, giving qualified citizens a voice according to "What is the Electoral College" Section 1. Maybe it's good because it allows people to influence electors, and history matters, says "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 15. "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16 says Electors should vote for the winner.

Senator, this winner-take-all approach is not right! All the focus goes to big states. They dump all their time and money into a few states. "Seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't see a single campaign ad," says "What is wrong with the Electoral College" Section 13.

Senator, shouldn't we go for the popular vote? People would be inspired to vote, knowing their vote directly impacts who gets elected. Power to the people!

It's amazing how many cars we see everywhere now! Folks don't bother walking or biking like they used to; they just hop in their cars. This definitely shakes things up, huh? Is this really what we want? Rosenthal, Duffer, and Selsky totally get it. They're all about fewer cars, but they each have their own reasons. After checking out their stuff, you see it: fewer cars equal more free time, better business, saving our resources, and less of that "car culture" stuff.

Are you always running late because of crummy traffic? Maybe hoofing it or biking would be faster! Selsky chatted about Colombia's totally car-free day. Millions "hiked, biked, skated or took buses," so the roads were "eerily... devoid of traffic jams." Less traffic means less waiting around! Walking helps Mother Earth and saves time, too. Businesses do great when there are fewer cars around. Selsky mentions "new restaurants and upscale shopping districts" popping up, and sidewalks becoming "broad, smooth sidewalks." More walking means more shopping!

Saving resources is a super big bonus of cutting back on driving. Rosenthal points out that cars cause "12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe... and up to 50 percent in some car-heavy areas." That's a seriously big problem! We *have* to drive less because those car fumes are hurting the planet. Some cities even have "no-driving" days. Duffer said that Paris had a "partial driving ban" after "days of near-record pollution." Less driving means less pollution and less traffic! Awesome.

That whole car culture thing might be on its way out. Studies show that young Americans are driving way less these days. Rosenthal mentions a "23 percent" drop in young drivers. Maybe it's tickets, parents, or just caring more about the planet. But Rosenthal also said experts think this will help cut "carbon emissions and the environment." So, car companies are changing things up, and tech is changing our commutes.

Bottom line: less driving means more free time, thriving businesses, saving resources, and a weaker car culture. Next time, maybe think about driving less. It's better for the Earth.

Okay, so listen, this Electoral College thing? We need to stick with it. Some people say it’s old, but it’s still the best way to pick a President, plain and simple. Think about it: elections are way easier than trying to count every single vote. Less chance of constant recounts! And here's why: it stops the big states from bossing everyone around. Honestly, the Electoral College keeps things from going totally bonkers.

Electoral College wins don't usually end up in those crazy fights you'd get if we just added up all the individual votes. A lot of times, candidates get way more electoral votes than the popular vote says they should. Obama, for instance. He got almost 62% of electoral votes, even though only about 51% of people voted for him. With most states using "winner-take-all," figuring out each state's electoral votes is easy when someone just barely wins, even if they didn’t get more than half the votes. A tie? Possible, sure, but super rare, even with an even number of total votes. Trying to count absolutely every vote? Total chaos.

The Electoral College actually stops us from needing to do runoff elections. Runoffs happen when no one gets more than half the votes. Look at Nixon in '68 and Clinton in '92, they only got around 43 percent of the popular vote, but they still won big in the College with 301 and 370 votes respectively. The College gives us a clear winner, so we don't have to vote again. Seriously, runoff votes are just a waste of time.

So, when you get right down to it: the Electoral College stops big states from having too much say. We really, really need to keep it around. Maybe there are better ideas out there, but the Electoral College is definitely the best system we’ve got right now.

*Click!* Mom's iPhone captured my face. It felt like I had superpowers just holding that little plastic thing. Ah, youth! I remember lying on my belly in the kitchen, pushing a toy car around while Mom cooked, the garlic smell everywhere. And finally, my driver's license! Huge for teens, right? But, it's not all good. Less driving means less pollution, and we can all breathe better.

Now that I drive, I hate the smog that makes me squint going to school. Ugh, my car makes it worse! Car fumes cause smog, shooting greenhouse gasses into the air. Apparently, U.S. cars cause about half of these emissions (Source 1). Even making gasoline pollutes! It's such a waste and releases tons of carbon. So, making the fuel releases greenhouse gasses, and THEN our cars release even MORE?! AP Environmental Science taught me that too many greenhouse gasses are BAD. They trap sunlight and UV rays, and then what? Global warming. Plus, smog is nasty. Paris was choking, so they banned cars to clear it. In one car-free day, the smog was gone! People were breathing easy (Source 2). Smog hurts Paris' tourism. Who wants to see Versailles covered in pollution?

Less driving helps our bodies and minds. In Vauban, Germany, cars are banned. Many people who moved there sold their cars and are happier because of it! Heidrun Walker, a media trainer with two kids, said, "When I had a car, I was stressed. Now, I'm happier." (Source 1). Walker's story shows that ditching the car helps your mental state. And your body feels it too! People are walking, running, biking, or skating everywhere. Bogota, Colombia, has a "Day Without Cars" every year, encouraging people to walk (Source 3). Walking is great for your heart! If more places had car-free days, maybe we'd see fewer heart problems.

So, less driving means cleaner air, healthier bodies, and happier minds. Next time you reach for your keys to drive to your friend's, stop and think. Think about your health, the planet, and maybe walk instead.

Elections truly matter! They're not everyday occurrences. Your vote wields significant power; it shapes our nation's course. Choosing the president *should* be straightforward: tally the votes, and voila, a victor emerges! Cast your ballot for your genuine choice. It's that simple. The popular vote is superior, devoid of Electoral College intricacies.

Many champion the popular vote's fairness. It's easily grasped: vote, count, the highest wins. Candidates strive to visit various states and engage with citizens, scheduling permitting. Yet, Bradford Plumer notes the Electoral College leads candidates to "bypass unwinnable states, prioritizing close 'swing' states." Unfair! Every state deserves candidate engagement. Certain candidates overlook entire states. Plumer highlighted that "during the 2000 election, candidates ignored seventeen states, like Rhode Island and South Carolina. Voters in 25 major media markets saw zero campaign ads." How can voters make informed choices without candidate exposure?

Moreover, the popular vote is more accessible than the potentially complicated Electoral College. Popular vote? Utterly simple: vote, count, most wins. Electoral College? Plumer explains that a tie results in "the House of Representatives deciding, states voting for the president. The Senate selects the vice president. Each state gets one vote, so Wyoming's vote, representing 500,000, mirrors California's 55 reps, representing 35 million voters." Envision the turmoil! A single vote holding immense sway! Popular vote's clear: everyone votes, the highest wins. No representatives needed to complicate matters.

Essentially, the popular vote prevails due to its inherent fairness. We select our preferred candidate, free from interference. And it's simpler than the Electoral College. A tie might involve representatives voting, perhaps without full understanding. Voting is crucial, so let's get it right. We should vote according to our desires, preventing others, especially representatives, from deciding for us. Voting truly matters!

Alright, so about how the Electoral College actually works? According to the Federal Register, here’s the deal: We cast our ballots for electors. After that, these chosen people meet up and vote for who should be the President and Vice President. Then, Congress tallies all of the votes. The bottom line is, we are picking people who then pick the big cheese. Seems simple enough, no? So why are people so worked up over this?

A whole lot of people are pushing to get rid of the Electoral College, and just switch to a simple popular vote system. Bradford Plumer brings up an important question of whether or not the electors will even vote how we expect them to. He doesn’t seem to think so! Maybe they will sometimes, but we’re still choosing electors and HOPING they make the right call. We have to put our faith in them. But, even if we voted directly for the President, we'd still have to believe THEY'D actually do a decent job. In the end, it’s pretty much the same thing, just with an extra step thrown in there.

Richard A. Posner makes a really interesting point: "Why get rid of something that we already dislike?" He's of the opinion that the Electoral College helps keep things fair, sort of like how the Senate supports smaller states. Exactly! Big states don’t have a lot of say in the Senate. Therefore, if we just went with the popular vote, those big states would get even MORE attention than they already do since that's where most of the people are.

Also, the Electoral College helps keep things stable in case no one gets more than 50% of the votes. Take '68, for example. Nixon had the most votes (43%), but he didn’t have the majority. And Clinton was in a similar spot in '92. Despite that, they both still won the Electoral College, which meant they didn't have to do a runoff. That was a close call!

Personally, I'm all for keeping the Electoral College the way it is. It does a good job of keeping states like Texas from pushing around places like Rhode Island. On top of that, it helps us to avoid runoffs. Sure, some folks say it's outdated, but I'm still convinced that the Electoral College is a reliable way to vote.

Alright, listen to this:

This Electoral College thing? Honestly, it’s the worst – old, unfair, and just plain ridiculous! Some try to defend it, but their arguments are weak. Are they scared of a simple vote? Really? Even Dole got it: it has to go! The reasons *for* it just don't add up. And check this out: electors can't be trusted! You *think* they'll vote as promised, but GUESS WHAT? No promises! Nixon, Carter, Dole, Chamber, even AFL-CIO – they all wanted it gone. People think votes should pick the winner, right? But we're picking *electors*, not the President himself. A tie? The House decides, but the votes aren't even the same!

Electors? They're not *really* voting straight for the president. Can we trust them to keep their word? Nope! Could voters pick *bad* electors? Yes! The Electoral College? It's broken, easy to mess with. People think they're voting for the President, but SURPRISE! It's not that easy. We're not directly picking *electors*! Why think they're loyal? In '60, Louisiana almost switched electors to hurt Kennedy. Crazy! It's easy to cheat. The base is wobbly. "Faithless" electors have broken trust before. It could happen again. It's rigged! Get rid of it! Let people vote straight up!

If the electoral vote ties, the House picks the President and VP. That process? Just as bad! Totally unfair. Not a real democracy. Each state gets ONE vote. Wyoming's one rep, with about half a million people, has the same power as California's 55 reps, with 35 million! Does that make sense? NO! That's how it is. People split votes, with one party for President and another for Congress. So, the House's pick could be way off. Ties are rare? Actually, no: 41,971 votes in '68 could have caused one. 5,559 votes in Ohio and 3,687 in Hawaii in '76 could have too. Small changes, BIG results. "Your vote counts?" That's BS. *This* system? Can't believe it's still here. People deserve the same say.

Seriously: ditch the Electoral College. Straight voting is the way. It's easy. Electors are not dependable. The House doesn't represent. Let people vote right! Let us vote! A President "For the people" should be picked "By the people."

Listen up! Driving is seriously bad for the planet. Can't we cut back a little? The Earth is suffering, and cities are being suffocated. Cars stink everywhere, from here to Europe and even Central America; it's a worldwide problem. Burning so much gas is basically cooking the planet. What if we drove less?

Paragraph 10 of "Paris bans driving due to smog" starts with "After days of near-record pollution..." Paris, with smog? How terrible! The "City of Love" can barely breathe! If Paris has smog alerts, what must it be like other places? We have more of everything – factories, cars, you name it! What are we going to do? If we don't do something, it'll only get worse. Let's wake up and act now!

In paragraph seven of "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," David Goldberg says, "All of our development since World War II has been centered on the car..." Why do we value cars over people? Sure, cars are fast, but what are we sacrificing for them? Forget gas; what about our health? Our money goes to roads instead of parks, and we're surrounded by malls instead of nature. Trees are great for clean air, but pollution is hurting them. Smog? Nobody knew what that was back then, but now it's all cars, all the time!

Okay, so we need to drive less, but it's not hopeless. How about car-free days? Drive, get fined! They do it in Bogota, Colombia. There's even an article saying, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." Maybe it could work here too! Cali and Valledupar are trying it out as well.

When last summer rolled around, I took a trip out to California, picturing it was going to be awesome. I was thinking surfing, rad mountains, the whole deal. When I got to LA, I thought it would be a city like Miami, full of life. But, man, I was wrong! Honestly, the gross smog and crazy traffic ruined everything. I know it is normal in cities like that, but less car use would help so much. Cleaner air and safer streets? That's a big win!

Elizabeth Rosenthal said that "Cars cause 12% of Europe's pollution... and it's as high as 50% in bad areas" (Rosenthal, paragraph 5). But trucks cause problems too. Since everyone knows about pollution, lots of folks want fewer cars, and countries like France, Colombia, and the US are doing things about it. And guess what? It's working! Rosenthal also said, "Studies show that fewer Americans are buying cars, not driving as much, and getting fewer licenses" (paragraph 29). Cars won't disappear, but even less use helps the world.

Imagine if there were fewer cars in places like NYC, there would be fewer accidents. In the US, there are about 500 crashes every day, and the world has over 5000! So many people die in car accidents, but we can prevent this. Sure, bad and distracted drivers make things worse. But get this: fewer crashes mean less pollution, too. Duffer said, "Traffic dropped 60 percent in the capital of France" (Duffer, paragraph 14). Plus, Bogota's Car Free Day is helping other Latin American countries cut down on traffic and make walking safer.

Everyone is starting to see why fewer cars are important. Less car use means better air and less accidents; that is what matters. After I left the city and the highway, I finally saw the real beauty of California!

What exactly pollutes our air? Cars are the primary culprits. Nevertheless, reducing car usage is a fantastic solution. Imagine a scenario: purer air to breathe, additional savings, and significantly safer streets for everyone. Are you prepared to participate?

Indeed, a high volume of cars contributes significantly to air pollution. Recall the car ban implemented in Paris during a major smog crisis? Voila! Instantly cleaner air. Reducing your reliance on driving also negatively affects the profits of major oil corporations. Conversely, consider cycling, walking, public transportation, skateboarding, or trains as more attractive alternatives.

And here's a notable benefit: your finances will improve! The expense associated with driving infractions can be substantial. Furthermore, the cost of car ownership, including fuel, maintenance, and penalties, can be very high. Diminishing driving leads to greater long-term savings.

Finally, let's explore the topic of safety. One research study revealed that shifting travel patterns—incorporating cycling, walking, and public transportation—enhances safety. It's straightforward; fewer cars lead to fewer collisions. Additionally, reduced car traffic creates a safer environment for cyclists, scooter riders, and pedestrians. There's also a decreased risk of impaired driving!

In essence, decreasing car reliance creates mutually beneficial results: enhanced air purity, increased savings, and improved road safety. So, what specifically inspires you to occasionally forgo driving?

It seems a lot of folks think vehicles are an absolute must. However, while we use them to get from A to B, they're actually terrible for our world. Plus, they somehow make walking or hopping on a bus look totally lame. But, hey, what if we just chilled out on the driving thing? Now there's a thought! Other countries are already doing it, so it's not a pipe dream. Fewer cars could equal a healthier planet, better neighborhoods, and way better public transport.

Cars are doing some serious damage to our environment. Take a peek at Elisabeth Rosenthal's piece, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." It's about Vauban, Germany, and how they're working to cut down on those nasty greenhouse gases. The article points out that cars are responsible for 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions. And in the US, where driving is practically a national sport, it's even worse, like a whopping 50%! That's a mountain of pollution, making our cities pretty unpleasant. Dialing back on car use could really bring those numbers down. Governments are getting in on the action too. For example, Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," tells the tale of Paris, France, banning cars because the smog was just too much. "After days of crazy pollution, Paris only let certain cars drive to clean things up. People with even-numbered plates had to stay put or cough up 22 euros. The next day, it was the odd-numbered plates' turn." And guess what? It worked! "Traffic in Paris went down by 60% after just five days of smog!" Even tiny changes in how we drive can make a huge difference.

Less cars also make cities way nicer. Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," chats about how Bogota, Colombia, got better by ditching some of the cars. "The car-free day is part of a plan that started in Bogota in the mid-90s. They built tons of bike paths, like the most in Latin America. Parks and sports areas popped up everywhere. They even fixed those awful sidewalks, making them nice and smooth. Rush-hour traffic is way better, and new restaurants and shops opened up." Cities are now thinking about people on foot, realizing that cars can be a pain and trying to make things better for everyone. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal discusses the US trying to drive less. Bill Ford, the big cheese at Ford Motor Company, wants to team up with phone companies to build cities where "walking, biking, cars, buses, and trains all work together to save time, gas, reduce pollution, and keep everyone safe." Lots of folks are working to make public spaces better for getting around in an eco-friendly way.

More and more, people are waking up to the fact that we don't need cars as much, so they're driving less. In "The End of Car Culture," Elisabeth Rosenthal mentions fewer people are getting licenses and buying cars. "America's love affair with cars is cooling off. The number of miles people drove peaked in 2005 and has been sliding down ever since, according to Doug Short from Advisor Perspectives." The article also points out that you don't need to own a car all the time. "The Internet lets you work from home and hang out online. Cities are becoming more attractive, so people are moving back from the suburbs. Phones and carpool apps make it easier to get around, like shared vans for commuting. So, people who stopped driving because of the recession might not start again." Now, it's super easy to find a car when you need it, so you don't have to own one all the time.

Driving less is a really smart move. With fewer cars, we can tackle big issues like pollution. Cities are changing to make public spaces better for everyone. Cutting back on our car habit is super important for making people's work, time, money, and lives better, both here and around the globe.

Okay, so I'm pretty young and haven't experienced everything, but it's clear the Electoral College has been around for a while. I can't even vote yet, but my opinion as a 14-year-old should matter, right? I believe the Electoral College needs to stay. If we just picked the President by total votes, chaos could quickly ensue. One area might be obsessed with a candidate, but the majority of the country might dislike them. It would be a mess.

Choosing a leader based only on popular vote could be a bad idea. According to Article three, paragraph 20, the "winner-take-all" method makes candidates focus on important states. These states decide the election, so their citizens are well-informed. But under a popular vote, anyone over 18 could vote based on superficial reasons. Young people might just vote for whoever their friends like or based on party loyalty. People might rush their choices without much thought; the Electoral College prevents that.

Also, a popular vote might elect someone who is only well-known in one region. Article three, paragraph 19, states that a President who is only popular in one area wouldn't be a good leader because one area can't elect someone by itself. Imagine if popularity was all that mattered. For example, if Rodney is very popular in the South, but he isn't getting more electoral votes, he doesn’t need to visit the South. If that’s all it was, voters elsewhere might feel ignored, and the President wouldn't care about them. He’d still not be “their president,” even if he won. The Electoral College fixes this. We vote for electors who then vote for the president. Winning the most votes in a state means its electors support them; that's fair. A popular vote unfairly penalizes people who didn't vote for the winner.

I understand some people disagree. Article two says, "Candidates don’t visit states they can’t win... Seventeen states didn't even see the candidates in 2000!" Candidates ignore states they don’t think they can win. I understand the argument for the popular vote, where candidates would visit every state. However, I don't agree. Just because a candidate visits all 50 states doesn't guarantee they'll win. The Electoral College ensures a state's majority matters, not just overall popularity. It’s similar to high school: the popular kid might not be kind, while some less popular kids are. Choosing a President based on popularity could ruin our country.

Mr. State Senator, the Electoral College is very important. A popular vote could result in a poor president or one who wins only because they are popular in one region. Please consider my points when discussing this.

Envision a far superior world where folks relied less on cars. Think of a healthier, safer planet for us all!

Seriously, think about fewer cars clogging up the roads. The very air we breathe could become oh-so cleaner! Wouldn't breathing cleaner air be a grand thing for everyone's health?

Now, some might resist the idea, but cutting back on driving truly benefits us all eventually. Picture constantly breathing in polluted air, how truly awful that would be for your lungs! Plus, less driving means less time at the gas pump. Saving cash is awesome, isn't it? You could use it for anything you'd like!

Cars drain your wallet! Don't forget those pesky monthly insurance payments and weekly gas fill-ups. And even if gas prices seem low today, they can skyrocket overnight and hurt your budget!

So, is all that driving really worth the cost to our earth and to your savings?

Wouldn't life be more relaxing if we weren't so attached to our cars?

Ultimately, the choice rests with you, though!

Do you ever stop to think about the pollution spewing from your car? Probably not! You're likely focused on the destination or phone, not thinking about the environment. It's easy to understand since environmental worries are often distant! We only seem to notice it when a startling news story appears about air quality.

Unfortunately, since the pollution from cars slowly builds up, the harm it causes to our planet is easy to miss.

If it keeps going, both plants and people might struggle to breathe and wind up in the hospital!

Consider a life free from stressing about car payments or constantly stopping for gas.

So many individuals and families are stressed about money, struggling with bills and gas just to make it to work daily.
