Instead of your car constantly burning gas, why not try walking or biking every now and then? Imagine all the money you could save from that!

Perhaps scrapping the Electoral College isn't such a great idea after all. The Founding Fathers put it there to give Congress and regular folks an equal voice. Now, some grumble that it's unfair because, well, technically, the electors are the ones who pick the president, not us. We're just voting for them. But electors switching their votes? That's super rare; it's mostly just chatter. So, despite the complaints, the Electoral College should stay. It simplifies stuff, lessens votes, and empowers smaller states more.

Alright, the Electoral College gets messy, like back in 2000, but that's less common than squabbling over who got the most popular votes. Fact is, a candidate can snag the Electoral College without winning the popular vote. In 2012, Obama grabbed 61.7% of the electoral vote but only 51.3% of the popular vote. When one person wins big in a state, even a small victory turns massive.

Plus, the Electoral College dodges elections where nobody gets over half the votes. Take 1968 and 1992, where the winners only had around 43% of the vote. That could mean ties and endless voting! But the Electoral College just picks a winner right away. Some even believe it prevents folks from skipping voting in states where their candidate is sure to lose, like Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. If it's pointless, why bother showing up, yeah?

Even so, the Electoral College balances things out, giving small states a fighting chance against the big boys. In 2012, Florida was neck and neck, and its 29 votes went to Obama. Candidates work harder in big states because they matter more in the Electoral College. People whine about the winner-take-all deal, but candidates target swing states instead of wasting time where the outcome is obvious anyway.

Sure, some might skip voting because they figure their vote doesn't count for much, but one vote hardly ever decides an election. People vote to show what they stand for, not because they think they are the sole, deciding factor. The Electoral College, not just the popular vote, should still pick our leader, plain as day.

Seriously, the electoral college? I don't get it. Richard A. Posner tried to defend it in his book, but I'm still not convinced. Remember Florida in 2012? Obama won all the electoral votes with only 62% of the vote. But what about the 48% who voted against him? Those votes didn't even matter! It's winner takes all, even if the election is close. Bradford Plumer is right—swing states are all that matter. In 2000, candidates basically ignored seventeen states. Only those swing states mattered. With a popular vote, everyone would matter, right? Every vote counts the same, no matter where you live.

And the electoral college could cause huge issues. Plumer says electors sometimes vote how they want. It's rare, but it could happen! One person could screw everything up. And what if there's a tie? Each state gets one vote? That's insane! So, people in big states have the same vote as someone in a tiny state? A popular vote would solve all of this.

Richard A. Posner tries to make the electoral college sound good, but his arguments fail. He thinks a popular vote would cause more disputes? Where is his proof? He claims candidates would only visit their home states? Maybe, but they'd probably lose. He says the electoral college teaches voters in swing states? Seriously? A popular vote would teach *everyone*. He thinks it helps big states? No way. Candidates will go where the people are, period. Runoff elections? Okay, he has a point. They could be a pain. But still, it's better than being unfair or causing a potential disaster!

It's nearly impossible to defend the electoral college. There's tons of proof that it's unfair and dangerous. A national popular vote? We need it, stat. If you like the electoral college? You're supporting unfairness, and maybe even a disaster. Nobody wants that!

The U.S. really likes freedom, fun, and going after what makes you happy! Everyone having a fair shot is super important here too. But, the Electoral College? Seriously? Certain people, who are supposed to stand for what their state picked, are the ones who really vote for president. Is that really how democracy should work? People are still arguing about it. Lots of people think it's messed up, and you can see why.

Imagine this: You vote like you really mean it. Someone is picked to vote for the person you like. Then, WHAM! They change their mind! Now you're stuck with someone you hate. That's not cool, right? Electors start to seem shady. Wouldn't it be easier to just count up all the votes from everyone? The person with the most votes wins, end of story, not this roundabout thing. It's actually happened: The person most people voted for LOST because of the Electoral College, and your favorite person is out of luck. Voting should be simple. We fought for it.

Besides not being able to trust electors, the Electoral College has other issues. It might seem like ties don't happen much, but there have been some super close elections. Some say the Electoral College makes things easier if there's a tie, but does it? If everyone votes how they want, ties should be easy to fix. One elector changing sides can change thousands of votes. Plus, electors get a lot of credit when their person wins, even if bigger things at the national or state level deserve the credit.

Basically, the Electoral College makes you wonder if things are really fair and if you can trust it. Regular people can't really control electors, and it's easier to fake a tie. Since America is all about everyone being equal, a simple, honest voting system makes way more sense.

Have you ever considered how our obsession with cars impacts the environment? Visualize this: air thick with pollution and rapidly depleting resources! Can you imagine a future where masks are required just to breathe because of the intense smog? But, if we prioritize "green" alternatives and drive less, we could revitalize our communities and aid the Earth! Envision cleaner air, vibrant public spaces, and innovative modes of transportation. Seriously, embrace sustainability and be astonished!

Ever wonder about all those cars and the terrible smog they generate, particularly in major cities? It's awful! Car emissions get trapped, leading to air pollution and breathing difficulties. Did you hear that Paris actually restricted car usage once due to extreme smog levels? They prohibited cars on certain days, which significantly improved air quality! Once, Paris's smog nearly rivaled that of Beijing, China! Guess what? After the smog cleared, car traffic resumed, and pollution returned!

Pleasant towns often have drastically fewer cars! Consider Vauban, a car-free community in Germany. Listen to this: approximately 70% of residents don't own cars, and around 57% sold their cars to relocate there! Heidrum Walter, a Vauban resident, says, "Owning a car was constantly stressful. Now, I feel much better." Additionally, Bogota, Colombia, aims to decrease car usage too! These car-free lifestyles greatly benefit our health and the planet!

Okay, okay, cars likely aren't disappearing anytime soon, but fortunately, numerous alternative transportation options exist today. In NYC, bike rentals are booming! Also, carpooling is increasingly popular for reducing street congestion! Interestingly, recent data indicates that fewer young people in the US are obtaining driver's licenses and purchasing cars. Envision a future with fewer cars!

Therefore, reducing our dependence on cars is a major victory for the environment. It can drastically decrease smog, and make the Earth a greener place as a result!

Goldberg from Transportation for America, a car-free group, says that after World War II, roads were mostly for cars. He believes we need new plans. The world is changing; cars, once key, are less so now. Let’s see why fewer folks own cars.

This is speeding up, like in Germany. Vauban, from 2006, is a great "smart planning" example, designed car-free. Now, more suburban folks walk, bike, or tram to work or school. "Smart planning" helps towns cut down on driving. In Vauban, 70% of families are car-free and happier, with less driving stress. A stunning 57% even sold their cars to live there! Car-free towns are popping up, making cities and suburbs denser and cutting travel.

A big reason is that we must fight global warming. Cars emit a lot of greenhouse gases, about 12% globally, and even 50% in car-heavy places like the US.

Paris had bad smog, so they stopped cars with even plates, fining rule-breakers, which cut traffic. The smog was from cheap diesel. Because it worked, Paris also banned odd-plate cars, since it's the capital and still smoggy.

Cars changed our lives big time. But, US studies show people are buying fewer cars, driving less, and fewer folks are getting licenses. Despite America’s car love, more want a greener world and see car-free perks. Insurance hassles, payments, repairs, safety, and car stress all matter. Cars are useful, but their downsides are clearer. Fewer teens and young adults get licenses, and more skip car ownership. Why even own a car?

Globally, folks are weighing car pros and cons, seeing the good in driving less, and changing how they live. One of history’s biggest inventions is starting to matter less.

Our planet is changing quickly! Folks are trying hard to cut down on greenhouse gasses. A major way to help is by driving less or ditching cars altogether. Sounds strange, doesn't it? But some places are nailing it, and it's paying off! They're seriously reducing car pollution.

Check out Vauban, Germany, near Freiburg. It's got zero parking spots, driveways, or garages! Instead, residents hop on a tram to reach Freiburg. Heidrun Walter, a media teacher with two kids, is a big fan. Vauban, all done by 2006, highlights a rising trend in Europe and the US: suburban living without constant car use. This compact area lets its 5,500 residents easily stroll to shops on the main drag.

Europe creates about 12% of the world's greenhouse gasses, but the US cranks out a whopping 50%! Take Paris, for example – the smog got so intense, pollution went nuts. The city banned certain cars. One day, cars with even-numbered plates were off-limits. The next day, it was odd numbers. Break the rule, pay a fine, but the city offered free public transport all week.

Bogota, Colombia, even has a "Day Without Cars" to get people to drive less. Millions of Colombians discovered alternative ways to get to work. This city of seven million urged folks to explore other travel options to fight smog. Mayor Antanas Mockus noted that even rain didn't deter people. That year, Cali and Valledupar, two more Colombian cities, joined the movement. Enrique Rera, the mayor of Asuncin, Paraguay, exclaimed, "These folks are kicking off something big, and others are joining in!" In addition to the "Day Without Cars," Bogota has rush-hour rules, improved sidewalks, and new dining and shopping spots.

Vauban's car-free setup, Paris's brief car ban, and Bogota's car-free day demonstrate how we can cut back on driving. People are eager to switch things up to lower greenhouse gasses. Car sales in the US are dipping, and fewer young adults are getting licenses – a 23% decrease from 2001 to 2009! The objective? Conserve resources, lower greenhouse gasses, and boost safety. With cities lending a hand, it looks like we can pull it off!

Want to cut back on driving? Rad! Initially, you decrease air pollutants by driving less. Believe it or not, cars are responsible for a considerable amount of pollution, in some regions ranging from 12% to a shocking 50%! Honestly, decreasing your driving is like giving Earth a good, deep breath, resulting in a healthier place for everyone!

Consider this, would you? Saying farewell to that commute could potentially decrease stress levels! The daily trip to work or school is often draining, and anxiety can kick in from merely thinking about the jam-packed roads. Intriguingly, there have been traffic improvements in certain areas from minor changes to rush hour. Ergo, opting for different commuting methods, or even shortening trips, can give everyone some much-needed peace and quiet within our chaotic lives.

The younger generations seem to be coming to the realization that driving less is, indeed, awesome. Info shows that fewer young adults are acquiring driver’s licenses. A recently conducted study displays that approximately 25% fewer young individuals drive in this day and age. What’s the meaning behind all this, you ask? Perhaps fewer collisions and a safer existence for all, specifically our youth!

To put it briefly, a multitude of positive results occur when someone chooses to drive less. From lowering pollution and anxiety to creating more secure conditions for the younger generations, choosing alternative travel methods over driving contributes to creating more beautiful and sustainable communities.

Word on the street is, big changes are coming down the pike! These days, politicians and average Joes alike are trying to drive less. Dig this: there's a town in Germany that's practically car-free. And guess what? The folks who live there seem really happy! But Germany's not the only one wanting cleaner air and less car stress. Even Paris, France is playing around with this "no-car" idea. Sometimes, Paris puts a stop to car traffic when air pollution gets too high. Get this, after just a week, the air quality gets a whole lot better! Both Vauban (Germany) and Paris are trying out different ways to cut down on car use. But hold on, Colombia has *three* cities doing the exact same thing! Every year, they have a "no-car" day.

So why did Vauban, in Germany, design a city with so few cars and parking spaces on purpose? Think about it for a second: most suburbs have houses spread way out, no stores or fun things nearby, and are packed with cars. Most suburbs are the same, it seems! But all those cars make pollution. Vauban fixed this by making it hard to drive a car on purpose. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote an article called "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." The article says cars make about 12% of Europe's greenhouse gasses. But, in some places in the U.S., it's almost 50%! Vauban helps clean up the air just by having fewer cars. They put walkways and stores before roads, making it easier to get around. The money they save from not building roads goes to other ways of getting around. Rosenthal's article says that usually, 80% of transportation money goes to highways. Only 20% goes to anything else.

Paris is really trying to change how people drive. There's tons to see in the city, and you can get almost anywhere on a bike. So, why make the city even more polluted by driving? That's why Paris sometimes stops cars from coming into the city. The pollution in Paris is almost as bad as in Beijing, China (which is known for being polluted). Robert Duffer wrote "Paris Bans Driving Due To Smog." At first, Paris thought about banning cars completely to stop pollution. But, it wasn't going to work because so many people drive. So, they found a middle ground. On some days, cars with license plates ending in an even number couldn't drive. The next day, it was cars with odd-numbered plates. This went on for a week. People who broke the rules had to pay 22 euros. Duffer's article said almost 4,000 drivers paid the fine that week! Even with the fines, there was 60% less traffic!

Bogota, Colombia, also limits how much people can drive. It's a city with almost seven million people. Every year, they have a day without cars. People walk, bike, skate, and take the bus. Andrew Selsky wrote "CarFree Day Is Spinning Into A Big Hit In Bogota." The article says the city wants to reduce pollution, traffic, and accidents by having people use taxis, buses, and other ways to get around. After three years of success, Cali and Valledupar (two other cities in Colombia) joined in. Selsky says the car-free day was part of a plan to make Bogota better that started in the mid-1990s. Because of this plan, they built 118 miles of bike paths! Plus, new parks and places to play sports.

Even in the U.S. (where everyone loves cars), things might be changing. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote "The End Of Car Culture." She says that in the last 8 years, fewer young people are getting their licenses and driving. Instead, they're walking, biking, taking the bus, or using taxis. As traffic gets worse, these options are often faster than driving. And when people do drive, they're sharing rides more often. This helps lower air pollution.

The Electoral College sure has its fair share of detractors. A Gallup survey quite a while back in 2000 highlighted a pretty clear preference for a straightforward popular vote system. Nevertheless, Americans ought to mull things over from all angles. Perhaps the Electoral College once served its purpose, but hey, times change, right?

Now, choosing electors, that can be a real headache and drag on forever with the Electoral College. States gotta handpick their electors first, then go through the whole shebang of planning and holding meetings. And come election day, figuring out who's eligible to be an elector and the ins and outs of it all can leave your head spinning. These very electors then cast their votes for the President and Vice President. After all that, the votes are finally tallied. With the Electoral College boasting 538 members, you're gonna need at least 270 votes to snag the presidency. Seems like a direct election could really cut through the red tape and simplify things.

A direct election? Well, now that's a game-changer. Every single vote would carry weight, and that might just bridge the divides between states. People crave that feeling of mattering. Given the Electoral College's waning popularity, many are warming up to the idea of a direct vote as a superior means of selecting the President and Vice President.

In each state, every candidate puts forward their own slate of electors, typically chosen by their respective political party. Now, if someone's not vibing with the selected electors, they might feel like their vote's just a drop in the ocean, like they're powerless to influence the presidential pick.

Bottom line? Folks are pretty fed up with the Electoral College. The complexities and drawn-out timelines just don't sit right. Some even suspect officials are clinging to it to safeguard their own positions. A direct election? That could clean up this whole mess and make choosing our leader a walk in the park.

Since the dawn of time, we've sought easier lives, crafting tools for hunts and building carts. Back then, horse manure posed a HUGE issue! Constant cleanup was needed. Suddenly, cars appeared. Great, right? Nope, they brought smog instead.

Recently, Paris restricted some cars due to severe smog. This threw Paris's weather off. Nights grew colder, days hotter. Some blame dirty diesel, but frankly, anything that burns fuel makes smog, naturally! But look at Vauban in Germany. Car-free! People skip cars because buses and trains rule. Vauban residents feel much less stressed than with cars. Kids play safely without cars, maybe spending less time on screens. Vauban too extreme? Well, Bogota, Colombia, has a car-free day yearly. Only buses and taxis pass; break the rules, pay big. Even in the U.S., young folks drive less. From 2003 to 2009, driving fell by 23%. Driving fades as more fun things emerge. A new trend? By April 2013, driving hit 1995 levels, when fewer had cars. NYC even has carpools, imagine that?!

Sadly, places like Florida lack good public transit. Homestead to Miami? Ouch! Drive to a bus, bus to a train, hour-long train, then another thirty-minute bus. Three hours EACH way. Thirty crazy hours weekly! Driving's just an hour, much better. Even errands in downtown Miami or Doral from Homestead can take all day by car. Without a car, waste DAYS, or wake up ridiculously early.

Looking at Vauban, folks seem happier than drivers. They socialize more through walking. Air's clean, unlike smoggy cities. Cars cause about 12% of Europe's pollution, over 50% in the U.S.! Had Paris acted sooner, maybe less smog would exist. Post-WWII, we heavily invested in cars. Imagine better medicine or other advancements with that money. In the U.S., highways get more funds than buses and trains. Vauban and Bogota work due to close shops and homes, plus good bus routes. Total car removal may be tough due to reliance, but we can change step by step.

Hey, check this out! I'm super worried about how nasty our air pollution is across the country. Here's an idea: what if we drove a lot less? Seriously, it could make a massive difference to the pollution problem, since cars are a major cause. Also, we could hang out more as a community and make our towns way better.

Cars are a seriously big reason for air pollution. If we drove less, there'd be fewer awful pollutants in the air. Less exhaust means less carbon messing with the climate. Guess what? That means breathing would be easier, and people would be healthier. Towns with less driving are already doing better. Also, more places are starting "Car-Free Day," like Columbia! Less exhaust also means less gross smog in the air. Cutting back on car fumes can stop problems like the crazy pollution in cities like Paris.

But get this, there's more! Driving less can actually help us hang out more together. Imagine walking around, talking to people, or biking to the park. People in car-free places, like Vauban, feel less stressed without all the traffic. Check this out: young people don't care about cars as much anymore! They like buses or ride-sharing, so they can hang out with friends and family more.

Think about this: all the driving means sidewalks and bike paths don't get fixed. If we drove less, they'd fix those old sidewalks and bumpy roads fast. In Columbia, "Car-Free Day" made them build new sidewalks and loads of bike lanes – over a hundred miles! They also built parks, sports fields, and cool places to shop. So, less driving can clean up our towns, make more parks, and create more fun places. Plus, it makes things safer by stopping car crashes and making streets safer for everyone.

So, think about driving less, seriously. It can make a real difference. We can enjoy cleaner air, be healthier, and feel more like a community.

The idea of cutting back on car trips, maybe by changing how we get around, is super appealing. Think about less traffic! Cities choked by smog, like Los Angeles and Beijing, could finally breathe easy. Drivers would feel less tense and have more cash – who doesn't love saving some dough?

Smog is a real problem in busy cities. You can almost taste it in L.A., and Beijing is often covered in it. Cars are a major source of pollution. In Europe, cars cause 15% of air pollution, but in the U.S., it's a whopping 50%! Fewer cars equal less pollution. If you need a car, consider a hybrid like a Prius. Some cities, like Bogota, have "Car-Free Days." People love them, and other cities are giving them a shot because these days really clear the air. When Paris had serious smog issues, they banned cars (except for hybrids and carpools). That shows how bad smog can get.

Driving can often be a major hassle. Traffic jams always make you late and put you in a bad mood. Drivers get angry when someone cuts them off and stress about accidents. Imagine if that stress disappeared! In Vauban, Germany, they've mostly gotten rid of cars to make life easier. Residents own cars but park them in a costly garage outside town (around $40,000!). You can walk to everything – stores, restaurants, you name it. Cars are mainly for long trips. Walking to get your mail or enjoying nature without cars reduces stress. Plus, you don't have to dodge traffic just to grab a bite! With phones and the internet, it's easy to stay connected without driving. Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram keep you in touch, so you don't need to drive as much.

Finally, fewer cars mean more money in your pocket. In places like Vauban, where there aren't many cars, you might not even need one. A good car can set you back $30-40k. Think about what you could do with that money! A beautiful home, an awesome vacation, or even your child's education. And everything is close enough to walk to. Sounds great, right?

If we just went with who wins the most votes overall, it could cause big trouble. The electoral college, though? It makes things easier and faster when choosing our leader. It's not perfect, sure, but it does the job way better than the other choice.

A popular vote has some good sides. Richard A. Posner says in "In Defense of the Electoral College" that electors, not normal people, are the ones who vote ("source 3 a15"). But if everyone voted, people would have direct power, cutting out the middleman. Do voters even know about these electors? Bradford Plumer notes in "The Indefensible Electoral College" that states choose electors, so voters don't know them ("source 2 a 10"). Doesn't sound fair, does it?

Even if voters feel lost, electors usually vote the way they should. Also, the electoral college always picks a clear winner. No ties here! Posner says, "The winner always gets more electoral votes than popular votes," so even a close race seems like a huge win ("source 3 a 15"). See how it works?

The electoral college also forces candidates to talk to different kinds of voters. Posner says, "No one area can decide the whole election, so the president must please everyone" ("source 3 a 19"). To win, you need to appeal to lots of different people. Swing states are super important and have smart voters. Posner thinks "People in swing states are in charge and know what's what" ("source 3 a 20"). Candidates better listen up!

Some might want a popular vote, but the electoral college makes the election process organized. It keeps things steady and makes candidates meet regular people by visiting many places.

Electoral College, right? It gets talked about a lot during elections! There are 538 votes total. You need 270 to win. States with more people get more votes. Big state like California has 55 votes, while tiny Rhode Island has only 3. Remember the 2000 election? Al Gore won more votes than George W. Bush, but he still lost. People really started questioning the Electoral College then. Some want to dump it, but I think it's important. It's key to how we pick our president. It makes sure whoever wins has a clear win, picks a president for everyone, respects voters in swing states, and stops tie situations.

If we got rid of it, picking a president would be a disaster. It's there so we always have a clear winner. If there's a tie, the Senate decides. Each senator gets a vote, so the small states get to say something. Also, candidates have to talk to different parts of the country. The president has to represent everyone, not just one place. That's why swing states matter a lot. Winning places like Florida, New York, Texas, and California can swing an election. These states have lots of people who really care about voting. In swing states, people pay close attention to what the candidates are saying. The Electoral College also stops situations where no one gets over half the votes. Like when Bill Clinton won with just 43% of the vote but still won big with 370 electoral votes. This avoids extra votes. So, we vote for electors, which some folks don't like, but it makes sure there's a clear winner, and that's important.

So, yeah, let's keep the Electoral College. It has 538 votes (need 270 to win) and tie-breaking rules. This makes sure we get a clear winner, picks a president for everyone, respects voters in swing states, and stops tie votes from happening.

Clearly, cars have a huge impact. Cutting back on car use can significantly benefit both us and the Earth. Clever city designs with alternative options can drastically improve things. Think: cleaner air, safer, quieter streets, and a more relaxed atmosphere overall.

Cars produce a large portion of Europe's greenhouse gases, about 12%. However, in car-dependent U.S. areas, this figure can skyrocket to 50%! Indeed, vehicles are major polluters, particularly in densely populated cities. One expert suggested that future suburbs should emulate Vauban's design not only in wealthy nations, but also in poorer countries, where increased car ownership among the middle class exacerbates pollution issues. The consensus among experts is that fewer vehicles directly translates to reduced air pollution. For instance, Paris implemented driving restrictions after days of severe smog to improve air quality. Fewer cars in cities means less smog, resulting in cleaner air for all and for the planet.

Sadly, car crashes are frequent and often result in fatalities. While not every crash can be avoided, it's possible to prevent many. Just think of all the lives lost in busy cities or as a result of driver anger! As one driver put it, "I was always so stressed while driving, but now I am much more relaxed." Driving can be stressful for many people. Driving less can reduce stress for everyone. Imagine a better, nicer place with kids playing and bicycles instead of loud horns, engines, and crashes.

Roads consume a lot of space. If more people walked, biked, and drove less, we would not need so many roads. Instead, we could enjoy wider sidewalks. Cities are dedicated to getting people to drive less, and some “… are working hard to make suburbs smaller…” Placing stores close together on main streets enables walking instead of long drives to malls. This approach improves safety for everyone.

Ultimately, reducing car use is a good idea. It reduces pollution and accidents while making our neighborhoods better and quieter. If cities and countries prioritize reducing our reliance on driving, the world will be a much better place, and our everyday lives will improve considerably.

Could it be that we depend too much on cars now?

Remember when Paris banned cars due to bad smog, like Source 2 said? The air was so bad, only some cars could drive! The smog stayed for days, almost breaking records! Too many cars make smog and hurt the planet. But, we can change! Driving less is good in many ways.

What if we lived without cars? VAUBAN, a town in Germany, did it! They want to see if no cars make people happier. Going to work can be hard with traffic, noise, and crashes. Sources 1 and 3 say, "70% of families don't have cars, and 57% sold theirs when they moved there. 'I was always stressed with a car. I'm happier now,' said Heidrun Walter." This means getting rid of cars can make you feel better.

Better air is another great thing about driving less. When the air was bad, Paris stopped cars to stop smog. Almost 4,000 drivers got tickets, and some lost their cars for a bit for fighting the tickets. But get this! It worked! Car traffic in Paris went down 60% in days. The air got better, so France let more cars drive on some days.

Are Americans tired of cars? Or do new tools mean we don't need to drive as much? Elisabeth Rosenthal says that "the internet helps us work from home and talk online. Cities are getting better, so suburbs aren't as appealing. Also, phones and ride apps are easy ways to get around, like vans for work" (source 4, 35). Tech helps us cut down on meetings by using apps, calls, texts, and video.

Thinking about all this, I think driving less helps us all. I hope you think so too!

Ah, vehicles! Everywhere you look, there they are. Red, blue, or even just plain black, they're always beeping and honking. Sure, they get us around. But what if they just vanished? Could getting rid of cars actually be a good thing? Turns out, it might just make the Earth and our minds a whole lot happier.

First off, fewer cars? That's a win for the planet, no doubt! Cars guzzle gas and spew out pollution like crazy. In Europe, they cause 12% of greenhouse gases. But get this, in some U.S. cities where driving is super common, it's a wild 50%! Paris even banned cars once to fight smog. And guess what? Traffic dropped like a rock – 60% in just five days! Less cars, less pollution, happy Earth. Plus, young adults drove less from 2001 to 2009, like 23% less! Experts think this might keep happening, which could mean even less pollution. After all, cars cause a HUGE chunk of pollution in the States.

But wait, there's more! Ditching cars could also make us feel way better, both physically and mentally. Picture Vauban, a German town with hardly any cars. One person there said they used to worry about cars all the time, but now they're just way happier. Less traffic and road rage? That means less stress, plain and simple. Bogota, Colombia, even has a "Day Without Cars," and everyone loves it. One person said it felt super peaceful. And guess what? Less stress equals better mental health. Oh, and get this: in Bogota, that car-free day makes people walk, bike, or take the bus to work. Exercise makes you happy! Your brain sends out feel-good chemicals. Fewer cars could mean more moving around, which means healthier, happier people.

So, to wrap it all up, saying goodbye to cars could lead to a happier planet and happier people. Sure, cars are useful, but they also bring a whole mess of problems with them. Next time you grab your keys, maybe think twice about driving. Think about the fresh air you're missing out on. Think about all the stress and anger they cause.

Ever stop and think why traffic gets so jammed, especially when 5 PM rolls around? The reason is clear: we simply drive way too much. Seriously, cutting back on driving? It's a total win-win and makes life a whole lot better, no question.

Okay, cars may seem like a quick way to zip around at first, but this perceived speed doesn't even come close to making up for all the horrible downsides. A huge letdown? Constantly driving turns us into complete couch potatoes. Walking or running is awesome for exercise, but we totally miss out on that when we're always stuck behind the wheel. My pal Walter confessed that owning a car used to cause him massive stress, but he felt dramatically better once he ditched it. We seriously need to chill out on the driving and find fun, easy ways to get around. Sure, walking might take a bit longer, but it's totally worth it. Wave goodbye to those nasty traffic headaches, and say hello to spots that cars can only dream about reaching. Plus, you can explore hidden treasures, not just the same old boring roads. Rain might throw a wrench in your walking plans, but Antanas Mockus, a former mayor of Bogota, insisted that rain shouldn't force us to stay indoors. Cars keep us dry, no doubt, but walking is way safer overall. Cities like Germany, Paris, and Bogota are trying to get rid of cars because of all these problems.

And another thing, less driving could lead to fewer road rage incidents. People tend to get super annoyed with each other while driving, usually from being way too stressed. If we drive less, we'll all be much more relaxed. Car crashes are serious business, and way too many people die because of them. Plain and simple, driving less could actually save lives. Cars cause way more issues than they ever solve. Walking and biking? Totally easier and safer, no contest. This would also clear up traffic jams, making it super easy for everyone to move around. Drunk driving is a disaster that steals lives. A tipsy pedestrian is nowhere near as dangerous as a drunk driver. Seriously, just think about all the downsides of driving way too much.

Finally, Barack Obama pointed out that we need to slow down on the driving to deal with pollution. Reports are showing that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less, and not getting licenses as often, which is awesome news. Paris straight-up banned cars because of crazy smog levels, preventing them from entering certain zones. Diesel fuel really ramped up the smog problem. In France, 67% of cars guzzle diesel, while only 53% do in other Western European areas. Bogota is aiming to "make other travel options more appealing and clean up the air." Violators get fined. They've realized that driving less lowers stress and makes the air cleaner. Bill Ford envisions cities where "people walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses all work together to save time, use less gas, reduce pollution, and be safer." These are just some of the many benefits of easing up on driving. Ditching cars entirely or even just driving less would be a massive game changer. Let's get rid of pollution, prevent crashes, and stop underage driving. Think about all the bad stuff about driving too much compared to not driving as much.

Okay, so we're still dealing with pollution and the Earth getting warmer. Why hasn't this been fixed yet? Cars? They're a HUGE part of the problem. If only people would just drive their cars less! Look at these spots making real changes. Like Vauban, Germany: zero cars! Then Paris, France? Only certain license plates get to drive on specific days. Bogota, Colombia is stepping up too!

Think about Vauban, Germany. Zero driveways or parking spots here! You have to pay a ton to park in garages. Apparently, cars cause about 12% of Europe's pollution. It sounds small, but it's not! If we create these car-free zones, pollution will definitely drop because people won't drive nearly as much. One resident, Heidrun Walter, shared, "I was always stressed with a car. Now, I feel much happier!" It proves life is simpler without a car. Less money spent on gas and car repairs means less money stress. Expensive parking in cities can save the Earth and people money.

What about Paris, France? Mondays: odd plates only, Tuesdays: even plates only. The pollution was once worse than even Beijing! But guess what? After they started this system, pollution dropped by 60% in just FIVE DAYS. Can you believe it? These car rules are effective, and they get results quickly!

We also have "no car days" that are enforced in Bogota, Colombia. Carlos Arturo Plaza summed it up nicely: "It's a good way to reduce stress and air pollution." He understands the connection between cars and bad effects. Cali and Valledpar, also cities in Colombia, are implementing these practices too! Even leaders from other countries are noticing and are very impressed. So, this idea could take off! It is a great way to make people drive less, that's for sure.

To sum it up, cars are a major issue. However, many people aren't considering the pollution they cause when they drive. Some excellent options are: paid parking in cities, odd-even license plates on certain days, and "no car days."

This nation, the United States, belongs to its people, to all of us citizens! We have the authority, the say, and it benefits us all. Isn't it wild that we cast ballots for our leaders? Many believe this is the ultimate method to choose the President and their trusty Vice President, isn’t it?

Nevertheless, here's a twist: the Electoral College poses a significant hurdle. While electors may possess deep knowledge of the nation, presuming to know what's best, they often fail to grasp the everyday realities of common folks like us. Regular individuals truly know what it's like in our daily lives. As Bradford Plummer keenly observed, "the best arguments for it just don't hold water in reality" (source 14). They might understand the ins and outs of Washington, but they overlook the struggles that many people grapple with.

It's paramount for individuals to actively participate in the government's affairs. If only the Electoral College had the power to select the President, regular citizens would feel alienated and disregarded. We desperately need folks to come together and keep things orderly. If that doesn't happen, there will be absolute chaos! Richard A. Posner aptly notes that individuals will "feel as though their vote counts for nothing, believing the new president disregards them and isn't truly their leader" (source 19). Imagine, people get upset when their preferred candidate loses NOW; can you fathom the uproar if they weren't allowed to vote at all?

The Electoral College system is simply unjust. Richard A. Posner highlights that some individuals favor it because of "its undemocratic roots" (source 17). The popular vote works seamlessly, so why complicate matters unnecessarily? Although change can be beneficial, it isn't always the case.

Ultimately, abolishing the popular vote would be a colossal blunder—completely unfair and detrimental to the nation. It flies in the face of the fundamental principle of "we the people."

Despite its downsides, the Electoral College still has supporters. Each state gets electoral votes equal to its members in Congress (House + Senate = Electors!). There are 538 electors total, and you need 270 to win. People who like it say it makes a clear winner more likely, assures that different groups are heard, really matters in swing states, helps bigger states, and makes recounts less likely.

Basically, the Electoral College tries to keep presidential elections safe and honest. It has a lot of power, but it's not perfect. The "winner-takes-all" thing, where whoever gets the most votes in a state gets all its electoral votes, can seem unfair. Candidates might focus too much on big states because they have more electoral votes. Also, you can win the popular vote but still lose the election if you don't get enough electoral votes.

A good reason for the Electoral College is that it helps find a clear winner. This should cut down on arguments about the popular vote, if electors vote as they're supposed to. Also, presidents have to think about what different parts of the country need, since no one area decides the election. People also talk about how it affects swing states, giving all votes to the winner. Plus, some say bigger states have an advantage, so candidates pay more attention to them. This all makes it less likely to have a tie and need another election.

Even though it has some good points, the Electoral College isn't perfect. Still, some think it's the best way to pick the president. They don't trust just the popular vote and wonder if voters are informed enough. Even with election rules, those who defend the Electoral College think it's better for the country. That's why it's important to know why people support it.

Senator, a big question: What if we changed how we pick presidents? Think about ditching the Electoral College. Instead, every single vote counts. Shouldn't the people, not a few electors, pick who leads us?

Big names like Nixon, Carter, and even the Chamber of Commerce wanted to get rid of it. Some worry that electors could change the results based on their own feelings. Isn't that unfair?

Remember Obama vs. Romney? Obama won big in the Electoral College, beating Romney by 29 votes. That shows how the system can be unfair and change what the people really want. A vote for all is fair!

Sure, the Electoral College can help when things are close, like when Nixon and Clinton won with just 43% of the vote. It stopped chaos then. But it gives too much power to things besides the people's choice. I think the American people should be in charge.

Senator, thanks for thinking about this. Do we keep the Electoral College, or do we switch to a system where the people really decide?

Dear Senator,

Some people dig the Electoral College. They say it forces candidates to visit swing states and talk to smaller communities (Source 3). But honestly, I disagree big time. To me, it feels like a super unfair system for picking our President.

Source 2 nails why it's unfair to voters. Because most states are winner-take-all, candidates only focus on close races in swing states, ignoring everyone else. Seriously, someone could lose the popular vote but still win the White House because of the Electoral College. Voters may not know the candidates true motives while they are in office. This makes you wonder if the winner even cares about the state. Source 3 even says candidates need broad appeal across regions to win. A candidate popular in one region, like Rodney in the South, would not campaign hard in those states since he would win anyway. If Mitt Romney ran against Barack Obama, Obama's popularity in the North would hurt Romney big time—that's the Electoral College in a nutshell.

Yeah, I still think the Electoral College is bad and unfair. Source 2 totally backs me up. It states that the Electoral College is unfair, outdated, and illogical. The arguments in favor of it are not true. Basically, the Electoral College goes against democracy and is messed up.

A lot of folks find the Electoral College a real head-scratcher. They say it's not fair and want it gone. Thing is, we don't pick the president directly. Instead, "electors" do the deed based on who we vote for. Some think this setup keeps leaders away from what the people actually want. Someone could win without getting the most votes because electors are supposed to follow what we want, but this weird way of doing things makes a lot of people want to get rid of it.

Plus, voters often don't know much about the electors they're choosing. Certain states don't get as many campaign ads or news, making it tough to make smart choices. Presidential candidates spend most of their time in swing states, trying to get attention and votes. Even though presidents should be talking to everyone, this focus can make voters in less important states feel like they don't matter. Even if you vote for someone, you're really picking electors, and winning the most votes doesn't mean you'll win the presidency, which makes people want a simpler way to vote.

Political parties pick "electors" who promise to vote for their candidate. The person who wins the Electoral College can still lose the popular vote, although that doesn't happen too often. Back in 2000, Gore got more votes overall, but Bush became president – the first time that had happened since 1888. Stuff like that could happen again, changing the results even if most people vote differently. There's a big argument because most states give all their electoral votes to whoever wins the state. However, it stops things from dragging on if no one gets a majority, and more elections are needed. The Electoral College is supposed to make sure there's a clear winner. People want their votes to count, but that's not always how it works.

A lot of people think the Electoral College is a complicated way to pick leaders. It was first made to get more people involved in voting, but it can mess up democracy by making individual votes less important. In a country that cares a lot about voting, making informed choices is super important. The way things work should make sure every single vote matters.

Dear State Senator, I'm writing to say that I really think we ought to keep the Electoral College exactly as it is. Switching to a popular vote system for presidential elections just doesn't feel right. The potential role of the House of Representatives in picking the president is super important, which makes the whole thing feel more just and well-organized. Plus, it helps everyone understand how we actually elect our president.

The fact that the House of Representatives might get involved is a big thumbs-up for the Electoral College. Source One, paragraph three, says each state gets electors based on how many people they have in Congress: one for each House member, plus two for the Senators. This makes sure lots of people get a say. Source One, paragraph five, points out that "The candidate's political party usually picks the electors," showing it's not just the House involved. The government is really involved in the Electoral College because those votes are so important.

Another great thing about the Electoral College is that it seems fair. Source Three says, "The Electoral College fixes the problem where big states have too much power because the Senate isn't fair..." This makes people trust the presidential election more. Source Three also notes, "If everything else is the same, big states get more attention from presidential candidates than small states..." The Electoral College really helps out the bigger states.

Most importantly, the Electoral College keeps us safe from election problems. Source Three, paragraph twenty-two, says, "The Electoral College stops problems in elections where no candidate gets the most votes." This makes voting simpler. The same source and paragraph also say, "We don't need more elections when no one wins the most votes; the Electoral College makes sure there's always a clear winner..." It guarantees a result, making us more confident in elections.

Sure, some folks might not like the Electoral College if their candidate loses, but that's not a good reason to get rid of it. It keeps presidential elections in order. Source Two, paragraph Ten, says, "With the Electoral College, we don't vote directly for the president, but for electors, who then choose the president." Some think the Electoral College is unfair because they can't directly vote for who they want. But electors vote for their state's pick. Source Two also notes, "Basically, the Electoral College is unfair. Because of the winner-takes-all system, candidates only care about close races in 'swing' states." Even with these worries, the Electoral College does more than just count votes; it helps our government pick the best president.

So, I'm totally in favor of keeping the Electoral College. It's good for both citizens and the government. It makes elections fair, stops chaos, and, most importantly, cuts down on problems so people are happier with the outcome. Every year, the Electoral College makes elections easier to understand and more organized. Even though the government plays a big role, individual votes still really matter. Individual votes are super important to the Electoral College, and so are government votes.

Does the Electoral College even matter anymore? It was useful a long time ago when states couldn't easily count votes. People voted by state, which was fine. But now, with better tech, it feels slow and unfair. If you're a Republican in a very Democratic state, your vote feels useless. Wouldn't it be better if everyone's voice counted with a national vote? Also, some states have way too much power because of the winner-takes-all thing. Small states don't get enough love, making it hard to know who to vote for.

It all started back when the telegraph was new! They needed a new system back then, instead of states counting votes and sending people. Just imagine getting votes on horses! So unsafe! Papers could get lost, people could get hurt, and some places might not be counted. The Electoral College made sense. But like the telegraph, it's old news. Now, phones can hold lots of votes. Computers can do math fast, giving us answers right away. We can send votes quickly and fix mistakes fast. What if we used a computer to count everything? Each place sends info to one computer, with checks and balances. Use multiple computers to stop cheating. It's a bummer that some people don't want to let go of the old ways! So old and not good!

It's just not fair. A Democrat votes in a very Republican area, but the Republican always wins. That Democrat's vote is like it never happened. Gone. Every time. But a national vote would make those votes count. They would actually mean something! The winner-takes-all thing makes it even worse. Winning California by a little bit means getting ALL their votes, ignoring what half the people think! Not right. Big states rule because they have more votes. What about the small states? Not much attention. Politicians like the big states more, ignoring the small ones. Voters in places like the Dakotas might not know much and just pick a party. The main battles are in the big states. The ads aren't always true, but voters figure it out. Swing states get lots of information. Small states? They are forgotten, so voters don't know as much, and their votes might not matter at all. A national vote would mean candidates would go everywhere, no more big-state bias, and voting would be fair for everyone.

So, here's the deal: The Electoral College was okay back then, but now? It's old and unfair. We can talk and travel easily, making it useless and not good for our democracy. It's time for a change!

Globally, folks are ditching their cars, like, everywhere! Duh, it's easier on your bank account and seriously cuts down on gross pollution. A ton of people want to make a difference, even if it's just helping their own town. Countries like China, Spain, and the U.S. are using cars less and less over time. While some aren't thrilled, most are stoked 'cause they hate pollution. Can you even imagine how much cleaner our air could be if everyone cut their car use in half? Yeah, cars are useful, but they also cause a ton of problems too, ya know?

If you roll up to Vauban, Germany, get ready for a surprise. You won't see many cars around. Sure, you can own one, but parking? Ugh, it's a nightmare and super expensive! Like, did you know it can cost forty grand just to park at your own house? Ouch! So, most people just leave their cars outside the area. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote a story called, "Life Goes On Without Cars in German Suburb." She says about seventy percent of families there don't even own a car. Even better, over half sold theirs after moving to Vauban. This really shows that dumping your car can be good or bad. But saving money? Totally! No car payments, parking fees, or crazy gas prices. Plus, the air is cleaner, too.

In Paris, France, the pollution was so bad they actually banned certain cars from driving. Robert Duffer wrote that if your license plate ended with an even number, tough luck, stay home! If you didn't, boom, €22 fine. The next day, only odd numbers were allowed. It actually worked, and pollution went down! Pretty rad for such a huge city, huh? Their goal? Cleaner air so people could breathe easier. Duffer mentioned that "4,000 drivers were fined" and they "impounded 27 cars." Traffic dropped sixty percent! As soon as the air cleared up, the ban was gone.

Bogota, Columbia, started with a tiny idea that blew up. Andrew Selsky wrote that "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota." Even though it was small at first, everyone loves it now.

Once a year, nobody can drive their cars in the city! Buses and taxis are cool, so you can still get around, chill. Seven million people are trying to breathe a little easier. They want cleaner transportation with way less pollution. Try to break the rules, and you're getting a fine! Selsky says Bogota has "118 MILES of bicycle paths!" That's a crazy amount of bikes! Parks got better, sports got greener, and the air got fresher. It's so popular that other cities are jumping on the bandwagon.

Rosenthal even wrote about the U.S. She said that "Recent studies suggest that Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses." Rosenthal 29. This really helps "Obama's ambitious goals to curb the United States' greenhouse gas emissions!" Rosenthal 29. And New York City? Always a taxi or subway nearby. Parking is a nightmare, so people skip cars altogether. Imagine the cash they save! Traffic is still awful, but picture how much worse it'd be with even more cars. It really shows the city relies on public transport. But it can get expensive, and without it, the air would be a total disaster.

So, what's the big picture? Fewer cars rock for everyone! They help fight pollution, clean the air, and save you tons of dough. Ditching your car? Win-win! If you can walk where you're going, do it! Walking is good for you, your bank account, and the planet. Cars might be faster, but think about the cost and the pollution. Try going car-free for a bit. See what happens and have some fun with it.

Business for cars is soaring, with companies raking it in! But, perhaps it's time to tap the brakes a bit on all that neighborhood cruising. Truth be told, studies show too much driving ramps up stress levels. Carlos Arturo Plaza pointed out in Andrew Selsky's "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," that driving less helps folks chill out. Less driving helps you out, and WOW, does it help everyone else too! Lots of Americans drive, but fewer are buying cars, which is interesting. Maybe they're thinking about carpooling, who knows? The bus might be slow, sure, but it's still a good choice! Slashing driving in our areas could seriously lead to some great stuff.

Cars are major polluters, messing up the air and the ozone layer big time. We all love how easy cars make things, and getting that license at 16? Awesome! But, we often forget how annoying owning a car can be, like ALWAYS needing gas. Imagine if we drove less – things would be way better! A smaller amount of driving helps the ozone layer, plus we all get to breathe a bit easier. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," nails how well these bans can work. Paris is super smoggy, so they stopped driving to clean up the air. Cold nights mixed with warm days trap those nasty car fumes, making pollution worse, which led to the ban. Afterward, the air in Paris got a lot better. The article noted, "Paris typically has more smog than other European capitals... Last week Paris had 147 ,micrograms of particulate matter PM per cubic meter compared with the 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London..."

Now, nobody's saying ditch your car for good, but think about the bad stuff it does. You might find it's more trouble than it's worth. Vauban, Germany, gets it, and they've totally changed how they live. Elizabeth Rosenthal's article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," tells how the town runs without cars. People there say they're happier without all the traffic and crashes. They did it by getting rid of street parking, driveways, and garages – who needs 'em without cars? Instead, Vauban made sidewalks better and easier to get around. One Vauban resident, a media trainer with kids, said, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Before, the government spent 80 percent on highways and just 20 percent on other ways to get around. But, things are changing over the next six years, showing they're planning better.

So, we gotta really think about how we get around cities. Less driving means good stuff: the government can spend money elsewhere, the air gets cleaner, we feel better, and we save cash. Is all that driving REALLY worth all the problems it makes? We need to ask ourselves that.

The roads in the US? Heaps of cars! Everyone loves to go fast, but not many people like to walk. Here's the problem: when everyone drives, the roads get super clogged, and it takes ages to get anywhere! Plus, cars hurt the earth and make the air gross. Less cars? Awesome! It's good for the earth and for us too.

Cutting back on car use? It's like finding treasure! It makes the planet healthier and makes us feel great. Did you know that one study said, "Cars cause 12% of pollution in Europe... and even up to 50% in the U.S. sometimes!" Since cars blast out nasty fumes, riding together or taking the bus really helps. And get this: walking or biking? It keeps us fit too!

That yucky city air, smog, comes right from car exhaust. It's full of gross stuff that makes you feel sick and makes asthma way worse, especially if you live in a city. Take Beijing, China – super smoggy city. "Paris even banned cars to clean up the air." Seriously, Paris did that to fix the issue. Fewer cars are like a "clean air" button.

But wait, there's more to it! Cars also mess with water and land. They guzzle gas straight from the ground. And digging up that gas? It can really hurt nature, causing oil spills that kill sea animals. So, jump on trains, buses, or walk/bike instead! That way, we don't need nearly as much gas. Cars eat up fuel, so less cars mean way less fuel needed overall.

People say that life is easier and calmer without a car. No more traffic jams or accidents! At a "Day Without Cars" thing in Bogota, Colombia, Carlos Arturo Plaza said, "'Less stress and cleaner air, hooray!'" See? Fewer cars mean less stress. Heidrun Walter said, "'I was stressed with a car. Now I'm much happier!'" She's happier in a German town where cars aren't a big deal. Less stress? Yet another reason to get rid of the car for good.

Some people only really need a car when buses and trains just won't cut it. As someone pointed out, "They walk, bus, or share cars," and "'A car is only for really tough trips.'" By skipping those trips when you don't need a car, we can make a better system where we don't need cars as much, and other ways of getting around get even better. Better buses? Fewer cars is good for the planet and for us.

Fewer cars? It's a win for the planet and helps you relax. One source pointed out, "Cars are the second-biggest source of pollution in the U.S.!" By skipping those drives and using buses instead, we can cut a huge amount of pollution in America. Less pollution, healthier people, and less stress? All thanks to fewer cars on the road!

People are realizing something: driving less is smart. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote about Vauban, Germany, in her article "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars." Vauban is a place with hardly any cars. Heidrun Walter, who lives there, said life is just better without them. No loud cars – instead, you hear kids laughing and bikes going by! The streets are lively, calm, and so clean.

Think about it: less driving could make our suburbs way better. Rosenthal's piece shows how cities have been getting denser and more walkable for twenty years. The idea? Smaller suburbs that are easy to get around in, with good buses and trains, and stores nearby – so we don't need cars as much. Even the EPA agrees this is a good idea!

The reward is big: cleaner air when we don't drive. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," talks about how Paris tried to fix pollution by banning some cars. And it worked! Traffic dropped a lot – 60% – after just five smoggy days. Pollution was like Beijing, China, which has really bad air. Paris proved that less driving means less pollution and traffic.

Andrew Selsky wrote "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in bogota," which adds even more support. It's about Bogota's car-free day, which encourages people to try different ways to get around and cut down on smog. The result? New parks, sports places, better sidewalks, restaurants, and nice shops. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a business owner, finds biking with his wife less stressful and better for the planet now.

The main point? Less driving is great for everyone! Less smog, less pollution, and easier to get to local stores. It makes our lives better now and helps keep the planet healthy for our kids and grandkids.

The wheel, a big deal, led to cars. Now, factories churn out tons each year, making travel easy near and far. But, gas cars spew out CO2, which hurts our Earth by messing up the ozone and causing acid rain. Also, too many cars cause traffic jams and other problems. Owning a car can also cost a lot. To save the planet, we gotta drive and make fewer cars.

Cars really pollute, spewing carbon dioxide, which speeds up global warming. In fact, Article 1, paragraph 5, says, "Cars cause 12% of greenhouse gases in Europe... but could be 50% in the U.S.!" Making and using fewer cars would really help the Earth. Less global warming means we can save icy places and animals like penguins and polar bears.

Traffic jams are the worst, often popping up out of nowhere. Passage 2, paragraph 14, says that "Paris traffic dropped by 60%" by stopping certain license plates on some days. Easier commutes make travel way better! That long three-hour wait turns into a quick twenty-minute ride, giving people more free time. Less cars and traffic should mean fewer crashes.

Car costs, whether you lease or buy, can quickly empty your wallet. Debt's been around since money began, affecting everyone. Article 4, paragraph 32, points out that "Poor Americans couldn't buy cars, and those without jobs weren't commuting." Spending too much on cars hurts both your money and the economy. Less car debt could really help the country.

In short, there are great reasons to drive less. Cars hurt the Earth by melting ice, hurting the ozone, and making weather worse. Less traffic makes moving around easier and frees up time. Cutting down on car making and leasing can boost the economy. Basically, fewer cars are good for everyone.

Okay, so the Electoral College, right? It's super old, and people get really mad about it. Basically, you don't directly pick the President. Instead, you pick "electors." Here's the catch: they *might* not even vote the way you want! Remember the 2000 election? The Electoral College put someone in office that most voters *didn't* want! Some say it stops ties, but that doesn't really make sense.

This really old system causes problems, just like in 2000. That election was a total mess! Getting rid of it could stop the same thing from happening again. Also, back in 1960, some racist people almost got Democratic electors in Louisiana to vote against Kennedy. Electors can change their votes, which is wrong. It doesn't happen a lot, but it's a *huge* problem when it does.

It's not just weird; the Electoral College is also not fair. Every state gets at least one vote, so someone in Wyoming (not many people) has the same power as a whole bunch of people in California (lots of people!). Some say ties are avoided, but remember 1979? A few votes in Ohio could have caused a tie! Because of "winner-take-all," candidates ignore a lot of states. Some states in 2000 didn't see any visits or ads, so voters couldn't even choose who to vote for.

A lot of people feel left out, especially after Al Gore won the popular vote but lost in 2000. People might not vote if they think their state is already decided, like Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. People aren't kidding around; their vote *matters*. Popular vote might cause ties, but it's still better than this messed-up system. More than 60 percent of voters don't like the Electoral College – that's a *ton* of people! We need to change things before they get worse.

It's really obvious: We've seen failures, close calls, problems, and it's not fair. People who support the Electoral College are wrong. The 2000 election and all the angry voters show how crazy it is to keep it. This system has been around a long time, and it probably won't go away soon. But now that more people see how bad it is, we can make things better.

So, if we lessen our car usage, it is actually a wonderful idea. We can get much cleaner air to breathe, new modes of transportation become more visible, and everywhere is safer than before.

Initially, when there are fewer cars on the roads, there is less pollution. This is certainly a great outcome for us and also the Earth. In Vauban, Germany, they are doing a pretty good job at it. Evidently, cars release a high amount of greenhouse gasses. In Europe, this accounts for about 12% of the total problem. In the U.S., it accounts for 50% of the problem. Places like Vauban, Paris, and Bogota are trying to cut back on car usage so that they can clear the air. Most experts believe that this movement away from cars is going to continue, which is a great thing for carbon emissions. Apparently, transportation is the second most impactful source of pollution within the U.S. Secondly, leaving the car at home gives way to cooler travel methods. Instead of sitting in a car, they can walk through parks, play a sport, or generally be more active and healthy. Also, there is less traffic on the roads. Bogota, Colombia, tried having a car-free day. People chose to walk, bike, skate, or get on public transport. The roads were very open and uncrowded. Paris even nixed some cars to help freshen the air, which led to a 60% drop in traffic. Public transportation was also made free from Friday to Monday, which shows how less driving helps everyone.

Heidrun Walter, who does not own a car, claims that she is much happier without one. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, assures that less car use lowers his stress levels and clears the air. Moreover, when there is less driving, there is also less danger. M. Sivak's research demonstrates how less young adults (16 to 39) are even trying to get their license, while older drivers continue to stick around. One study even found that there was a 23% decrease in driving among young adults from 2001 to 2009. This travel change is a great upgrade, and a win-win for everyone. Miles driven are nearly 9% less than their previous peak, and fewer people own cars now compared to the days before the recession. Because of this new change, people may not go back to relying on cars as much as they once did.

Overall, decreasing the amount that we drive is an excellent method for benefiting the planet, discovering new travel methods, and promoting community safety.

Ugh, traffic, right? A total drag, that's for sure. Whether you're behind the wheel or not, you're stuck in it somehow. Cars used to be the only way to go, plain and simple. But now, with all the smog, fewer folks are driving their cars so much, get it? It'd be awesome to drive less – fewer headaches, fresher air, and just feeling better all around!

Picture this: way less stress without being stuck in traffic and always in a hurry. We could hop on a bike, catch the bus or train, or simply stroll around! Take a peek at Vauban, Germany. Research suggests that it's mostly car-free, and folks there seem way more chill because they can walk just about everywhere.

Plus, cars are really not great for our planet and seriously foul up the air we breathe. In Europe, vehicles cause around 12% of global warming emissions! And in the US, some car-packed areas crank out as much as 50%! Paris is a good example of how nasty cars can be; one study showed its air got as polluted as Beijing's! They even had to say "no cars allowed" for a bit to clear the air.

Fewer cars are a win-win for us and our planet. Another study pointed out that Bogota, Colombia, has a car-free day each year. This really kicks up the air quality, and the people dig it! Even in the pouring rain, they show up for bike events. It was such a smash hit that two other Colombian cities are giving it a whirl. Bogota even rocks the biggest network of bike paths in Latin America – about 118 miles! Besides the government getting involved, regular people are driving less on their own. Word on the street is that driving is down by about 9%. This could be because more folks are working from home, so they don't have to drive so much.

So, things are shifting, and perhaps it's for the best. Cars don't have quite the grip they used to, meaning less yuck in the air, less stress, and a better life for everyone!

So, in Britain, if a party wins the most seats, they pick their leader. But, when people vote directly, everyone gets to speak their mind. This is the key difference of direct democracy, versus how things work in Britain. Leaders are sometimes good at getting people on their side, but they might not always be great, so preventing them from misusing their powers when we vote is very important. To keep things equitable, and to ensure the people truly have control, the U.S. ought to keep the Electoral College around.

Because of insightful founders, the U.S. has been a republic for ages. The Electoral College originally came from both Britain AND the Iroquois, though the Iroquois part is often forgotten because of prejudice. Similar to how British politicians choose the Prime Minister, the founders of the U.S. wanted their own method of electing a leader. They were afraid that if a President was voted for directly by the people, he could become like a dictator. Therefore, they made sure that the President had to listen to Congress, who could get rid of a President if they acted improperly. View the Electoral College as a guard against despots. For example, Hitler was never actually elected. And Mussolini became popular. With everyday people, the Electoral College prevents that situation. It isn't the best, but it does represent people. Richard A. Posner said it’s undemocratic because electors, not the actual people, are casting the vote. Every government is flawed, unfortunately. Even when they want to do well, some folks desire to get rid of the Electoral College. Even Nixon, who resigned due to scandal, wanted to get rid of it. Critics, like Bradford Plumer, view it as old and prejudiced. However, the Electoral College ensures elections are safe, and, by having electors for each state, it makes sure everything is equitable for everybody. That's representative voting for you.

The Electoral College represents everyone, carefully made by America's founders. Even with all of the disagreement, presidents that we admire, like Kennedy, Roosevelt, Lincoln, Washington, and Jefferson, were all elected due to it. To prevent against tyranny and to let the people have power, the U.S. has to keep the Electoral College.

I gotta say, Senator, this Electoral College thing feels super old-school. Shouldn't we, the people, get to pick our president directly? I mean, the way it is now is just plain unfair and broken. Life's already tough enough, so we need a fair country, especially with all that debt we're carrying. And honestly, Obama didn't exactly help things. It's just not right that where you live changes how much your vote counts.

These electors have way too much power, making our votes feel like they don't even matter. We vote for them, yeah, but then they do their own thing. That's a big betrayal of trust in the government. Shouldn't we, the voters, get to decide what happens to the Electoral College? I bet a lot of people feel the same way. Okay, I might be young, but my voice should still count.

Plumer got it right: we don't actually vote for the president. Remember back in 2000, when Gore got more votes but still lost? If we're all about equality, why do these electors get special treatment? It makes our votes seem less important. If the electors decide who wins, what's the point of voting at all?

Some folks say the Electoral College stops one group from taking over. But the way each state does winner-takes-all decides everything. So, who are these electors, and why are they so special? I may be young, but I'm not dumb. Why ignore what I have to say? America's built on a lie instead of fairness. We think we're in charge, but really, the politicians and the Electoral College are pulling the strings.

America's gonna keep going downhill unless we make some changes. Getting rid of the Electoral College is a huge first step. Why should you care what I think, since I'm just a kid? Because I know what it's like to be ignored, even when you know what you're talking about. America knows what it needs. We can't let a few people decide everything. Let us pick the president directly, even if it's just for a bit. If it doesn't work, we can go back to the old way. But we gotta try it to know.

Okay, so cars, right? Most folks kinda just shrug 'em off, but hold on! Turns out, they matter a LOT. Sure, we see 'em all the time, but loads of people can't afford 'em or even get behind the wheel. Take Vauban, Germany. They straight-up axed cars! They've got a train to Freiburg and roads around the edge, but nada inside. You CAN own a car, but parking? Forget about it. Next to none, and crazy expensive. Buying a spot in a garage way out? A wild $40,000 extra! So, check this: 70% of people there DITCHED their cars. More than half SOLD 'em after moving there! Heidrun Walter, a media trainer and mom, put it perfectly: "I was stressed with a car. This is SO much better." Vauban, since '06, shows us the future: suburbs WITHOUT cars. Pretty neat, huh?

Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote this article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," and she hit the nail on the head. Cars are cool and all, but HELLO, AIR PROBLEMS! They're messing up the Earth! Paris got so smoggy, they had to BAN drivers for a bit. One Monday? Even license plates had to STAY PUT. Or cough up 31 bucks. Next day, maybe odd numbers? The air was THAT BAD because the weather trapped all the car fumes. Insane! Traffic tanked – 60% in FIVE DAYS! The air was like Beijing. When they blocked half the cars, the air cleared up FAST. Odd numbers drove the next day.

Bogota, Colombia, had a NO-CAR DAY. Walk, bike, skate, bus – whatever floats your boat! No cars. End of story. And guess what? ZERO ACCIDENTS! And they do it EVERY YEAR. This was year three! They figured seven million people would use something else. It rained, but who cares! People still showed up! Mayor Antanas Mockus said, "Rain didn't stop a soul!" Everyone felt chill, and the air was cleaner, too. Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, biked with his wife. "It's good for stress and pollution," he said.

So, in the US, cars are a BIG DEAL. But…are we over them? Some think so. We're huge with tons of suburbs, so yeah, we love cars. Model T, Detroit, "Mustang Sally" – it's American as apple pie. But maybe we're shifting gears. By 2013, driving was down almost 8%. That's like going back to '95! Money was tight, and fewer people drove to work. But Michael Sivak at Michigan said something else was up. "Car ownership dropped BEFORE the economy tanked." Woah. We need other ways to get around, so people don't just go car-crazy again. New York's bike rentals, tolls, car rentals? Good start. One study showed young folks drove 23% LESS between 2001 and 2009!

Folks want fewer cars for reasons: they want to be safe, save some dough, and help out the planet.

We drive to work, school, sports, and events every week, many of us do. Driving seems simple, but too much is bad. It might seem hard to drive less, but it really helps a lot! It makes the air cleaner, makes us feel healthier, and just makes us happier too. That's exactly why people in charge really want us to cut back on driving.

Robert Duffer said that in 2014, Beijing's air was super dirty because of cars (source 2, paragraph 14). Paris had the same issue, so they stopped some people from driving on certain days if their license plate ended with a certain number. If they broke the rule, they had to pay a fee (source 2, paragraphs 10-19). The air got better super fast, so they stopped the rule. It really shows that driving less really works! Elizabeth Rosenthal wrote in 2009 that cars make the air worse in Europe and the US too (source 1, paragraph 5). So, when we drive less, the air is cleaner and we don't burn as much gas at all.

Driving to work seems so easy, but just think how much better you'd feel if you drove less! Rosenthal wrote about Vauban, Germany, where most families don't even own cars (source 1, paragraph 3). Heidrum Walter lives in Vauban and she doesn't have a car, and she says she's way less stressed because of it (source 1, paragraph 3). Andrew Selsky said that in 2002, Bogota, Columbia, didn't allow cars for a whole day (source 3, paragraph 21). Carlos Arturo Plaza, who lives in Bogota, felt much more relaxed and the air was also better. This just goes to show that driving less makes us feel way better. So, let's do it for our well-being!
