A ton of people want us to drive less because it's such a great idea and is very impactful! The EPA even helps towns that want fewer cars in general. Leaders are also helping out where they can (source 1, paragraph 9). David Goldberg says that the way we built things after World War II makes us use way too many cars, and we need to change that fact. Mimi Sheller says that things are already changing a lot, so it is only a matter of time (source 4, paragraph 35). Everyone actually agrees that driving less is a generally good and very positive thing. Let's all try our best to drive less often, for us and for the world!

The world is changing at a very fast pace! Young people are driving less and less than they used to (source 4, paragraph 41). Now is absolutely the perfect time to switch from cars to buses, bikes, or trains for transport. Driving less makes the air cleaner and helps us feel way healthier overall. The EPA wants us to drive less for all of these reasons, and wants to share all of this information with us. We definitely need to do it now to protect the future of the environment, of people, and so on. Driving less is a very good, helpful, and overall positive thing for everyone around the world! So, let's make it happen and create that future!

Senator, with all due respect, allow me to elaborate! I'm just a student, not exactly a political whiz, I confess. But I genuinely believe sticking with the Electoral College is the way to go. Wouldn't you agree it streamlines the whole presidential selection process?

The Electoral College boasts 538 electors, and snagging 270 votes seals the deal. We cast our ballots for electors, naturally, but our true intention is to rally behind a candidate's vision, backing their comprehensive platform. The current system elevates swing states to paramount importance, a feature I find advantageous. Echoing "In Defense of the Electoral College," these states gain heightened engagement. This could preempt prolonged election sagas, while safeguarding the significance of smaller states.

Numerous states employ a "winner-take-all" approach, awarding all electoral votes to the state's victor. Alternatives like "proportional representation" exist, but I have a preference for "winner-take-all." Though elements of the Electoral College might seem peculiar, this aspect simplifies matters considerably. Consolidating a state's votes for a single candidate cultivates stability, a quality absent in proportional representation, potentially extending election resolutions.

Ultimately, the Electoral College has stood the test of time. "What Is the Electoral College?" points out that the founders established it as a compromise. They steered clear of congressional presidential appointments, but also avoided pure popular vote. This historical context is pivotal. Thank you for considering my perspective.

Listen, Senator,

I really think we should just get rid of the Electoral College and go with the popular vote. You know, whoever gets the most votes, wins! It just seems like the fairest way to pick our leader, right? I mean, this whole thing with voting for electors, and then *they* pick the president? It feels kinda shady, to be honest. Shouldn't everyone get a say in who the president is, not just those "in the know"? I think it's time to rethink this whole voting thing.

Seriously, why even have electors? That article, "The Indefensible Electoral College" (source 2), is spot on – it's totally rigged! Candidates just spend all their time in swing states. But then, "In Defense of the Electoral College" (source 3) says that one region can't win it on its own. But the Electoral College kind of screws over some places; their votes don't even matter, so the president can just ignore them.

And another thing, *everyone* should get a vote, not just citizens. Shutting out tons of people just because they're not citizens? That's not right at all. Some areas always get the short end of the stick with electors, making some people's voices way louder than others. Source 3 says voters in swing states care more because their votes actually matter. But, come on, is it right to privilege "thoughtful voters" when other folks want to participate too?

Okay, I get it, the Electoral College *does* have some good points. Voting for electors is pretty straightforward, I guess. And if it's a tie, the House decides, with each state getting one vote – so, at least there's a system in place to deal with problems. I know, the popular vote might have its own issues, but we can figure it out. The Electoral College can also mean some areas are shortchanged when it comes to electors.

Basically, the United States should just pick the president by popular vote. It could really change everything. Everyone would feel like they matter and that they're part of something bigger.

Cars get us places, no doubt. But when we lean on them too hard, things go south. The planet suffers, and people get super stressed. So, cutting back on driving? Could be a win-win, really.

Fewer cars mean cities can breathe again. Cars spew nasty stuff into the air, making it filthy. One report says "cars are about 12% of Europe's greenhouse gases, maybe 50% in the U.S." It shows cars hit us hard. Paris, jammed with cars, almost choked on smog. Reuters noted, "Paris hit 147 micrograms of stuff per cubic meter, Brussels had 114, and London had 79.7." Just banning cars briefly cut the smog, proving exhaust is awful for the earth.

Less car use also cuts stress. Vauban, Germany, a car-free zone, is so chill. Heidrun Walter said, "I was tense with a car. Now, I'm way better!" A great reason to lose the car, huh? Bogota, Columbia, had a "Day Without Cars," a huge hit. Andrew Selsky said cool things happened, "bumpy sidewalks became smooth" and "rush hour rules slashed traffic". Less driving, less stress, that's it.

Cars help, but they're also a problem. Pollution fuels climate change, and traffic boosts stress. Less car use helps build a nicer world.

Dear Senator, The Electoral College really has me thinking. I'm writing you 'cause I'm worried, because I don't fully understand this system for picking our leaders. While it may have some good sides, I feel the bad outweighs it, you know? In my opinion, the whole thing is a mess, and it's time we got rid of it.

While a tie hasn't happened yet, it's still a real possibility with 538 electors! History tells us even small changes can change who wins. Richard A. Posner pointed out that because 538 is an even number, ties *can* happen, even if it's rare. A handful of votes causing a tie is not ideal. Just imagine if a couple of votes switched in Ohio and Hawaii back in '76, and we ended up with a tie! Numbers matter, sure, but when compared to the entire country's votes, they're nothing. Tie issues still concern me, even if each state's electors is based on population. The simple fix to all of this is just counting the popular vote!

Electors themselves could stir things up, too, and we all know how messy politics can be! Think back to 1960, when personal fights almost led Louisiana to choose electors who *wouldn't* vote for John F. Kennedy! Posner notes that parties pick electors who they think will vote for their candidates, and usually they do. But still, you can win the electoral vote and still lose the popular vote! Electors get to vote however they feel, and that just doesn't feel right. Voters might then think their vote just doesn't matter. Even if one vote isn't a huge deal, candidates should still be trying to win over everyone! Candidates often skip campaigning in states they know they'll win, and no one place can choose a president all by itself. This might make people not care as much. If the person with the most votes wins, people will feel more valued, and more of them would vote!

Remember the 2000 election? What a controversial time for the popular vote. One state's votes pick the electors, and this is what creates issues. When a candidate wins more votes, it can help them, sure, but it can also bum out those who voted for the other person. Posner talks about runoff elections in his writings. Without most of the votes, things get tough, and the Electoral College has to step in.

After everything, it seems unfair because of all the issues. The United States needs a way to pick presidents that is fair to everyone, where every vote matters! Close election fights, broken promises, and ties can happen because of this system. Every person would be heard if we switched to a popular vote! Thanks for listening.

Respected Senator, many of us strongly believe the Electoral College is important and must remain. Yes, it has problems, but it significantly benefits both the states and the nation. The true desires of citizens appear in the votes, based on input from all. Thus, this system gives votes more weight and authenticity.

First off, regular folks select the people in the Electoral College. This means individuals with similar views can voice their opinions. It guarantees the votes reflect the majority's wishes. Each state's opinion matters, according to its size. These electors usually understand our needs. If chosen well, they'll protect the state. So, the Electoral College ensures far more precise votes.

It's true that some claim it poses risks, like electors breaking promises. Yet, this always happens! Even presidents do it; not all fulfill promises. Trust is also key for the Electoral College. You must think that electors will perform their duties, just like trusting the president. Voting hinges on this.

Lastly, just counting them up wouldn't make votes as clear as the Electoral College. Imagine how awful a tie would be! This system can prevent a tie, and it may even aid a state or party. While luck plays a role, that’s better than only fate. The Electoral College makes voting stable.

In closing, we should keep the Electoral College. The whole system gets tougher, is more precise, and demands no more faith than other types of votes.

Automobiles swiftly rose to prominence as the go-to for lengthy trips. Today, legions of commuters pilot their vehicles to their workplaces daily. Laziness burgeons as people opt for cars over bikes or skates even for short hops! Nobody wants to hoof it to the corner market; instead, they hop in their rides. This driving is a major source of airborne gunk. It ravages our health and heats up the planet terribly. So, driving less is a victory.

A small dip in driving frequency yields cleaner air and some much-needed physical activity. Consider Vauban, nestled near France and Switzerland – they completely axed cars, eliminating street parking, driveways, and garages! Many families abstain from car ownership, and many sold their vehicles prior to moving there. Heindrun Walter, a resident, relishes the tranquility, children frolicking, and the cyclists!

Paris, infamous for its smog, is also aggressively pursuing car reduction. "The smog choked, so Paris legislated restrictions on certain vehicles to ameliorate air quality." These regulations led to "Traffic markedly improved in Paris after five smog-laden days..." If car use diminishes, alternative transit options such as biking or strolling become necessary. This promotes fitness and mitigates risks of cancer, diabetes, and heart disease. Shrinking car dependence can also curb global warming, prevent floods, and defend the ozone layer!

"BOGOTA, Colombia—Millions commuted via bikes, feet, skates, or buses on a car-free day, and the roads were DESERTED!" It's a fantastic approach to exercise while traveling! Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, notes that cycling with his wife relaxes him and benefits the environment. Professor Mimi Sheller perceives this as a significant societal evolution. The world shifts, profoundly affecting our well-being and the planet.

Less driving equates to cleaner air and healthier populations. If we curtail both the construction and operation of cars, folks can embrace biking, skating, or simply walking. The moment for transformation is upon us. Numerous cities are already spearheading this change, and it is essential that we partake, gradually.

The Electoral College, the American method for choosing presidents, is a subject of heated discussion. Opinion polls reveal that roughly 60% of Americans would prefer a direct popular vote. However, the Electoral College still has staunch supporters. The possibility of a candidate winning the presidency without securing the majority of individual votes is a point of contention, raising questions about the system's inherent fairness.

Each state's number of electoral votes, ranging from a minimum of three to a maximum of fifty-five, depends on its population size. A candidate requires 270 of the 538 electoral votes to win. The "winner-take-all" system, where the candidate with the most votes in a state receives all of its electoral votes, is generally employed, with the exceptions of Maine and Nebraska. The Electoral College can create a sense of voter disenfranchisement in states with consistent voting patterns. Proponents of the Electoral College claim it ensures a decisive victor and prevents candidates from focusing solely on densely populated urban areas. It also serves as a safeguard in the event of a close popular vote.

Although initially intended to protect smaller states, the Electoral College no longer effectively serves this purpose. Candidates tend to concentrate their efforts on swing states to maximize their electoral vote count. Predictable states are often overlooked because their voting outcomes are largely known. One must consider if the Electoral College should be maintained if it no longer functions as intended.

Despite its long-standing presence and general success in determining winners, the Electoral College may not be the optimal method for electing presidents. It has the potential to result in a tie. Some argue that the focus on swing states encourages greater voter awareness due to the increased significance of their votes. Yet, abolishing the Electoral College would simply redirect candidates' attention to different regions. The disparity in the value of individual votes remains a concern, leading many to advocate for the elimination of the Electoral College as the most equitable solution.

Esteemed Senator,

Everyone is talking about the Electoral College, from average folks to big-shot politicians. This system, which picks our president, tries to make sure everyone's vote matters. When our country started, it was meant to balance the government and what the people wanted. Getting rid of the Electoral College? That would mess everything up! Keeping it means we have a working government and people who care.

The Electoral College might seem weird, but it makes things fairer. To win, candidates have to talk to different groups all over the country. Like Source 3, line 19 says, candidates need to "appeal to different regions." Just being popular in one area isn't enough. This balance stops one place from having too much power. It makes voters pay attention and know their vote is important.

Our country is all about fairness, and the Electoral College shows that. Source 1, line 1 says, "it's a process...the founders made it up to find a middle ground." It was created to make sure elections are fair, and it shows why balance is important.

Also, the Electoral College usually makes sure the winner has support from many people. Source 3, line 22 says that, "it stops elections where no one gets most of the votes." It's a simple way to pick the president, and it's super important. We usually don't have to worry about "electors" causing problems. After all, if a candidate doesn't have lots of support, they can't win.

In the end, the Electoral College is key to keeping our country stable. We count on it to be fair. Getting rid of it could hurt our country, its future, and its people.

Wouldn't just axing the Electoral College make things easier? It feels clunky compared to just counting up all the votes, right? Whoever gets the most should win, period! Seriously, though, the Electoral College feels like a relic. Huge problems erupt when electors go off-script and ditch the popular vote. Sure, some folks defend it, but honestly, the headaches outweigh any good it does.

It's such a roundabout process. We're not even voting for the president directly! Instead, we vote for these electors who then vote for the president. If a party wins a state, bam, they snag every single electoral vote from that state. Imagine Florida tossing all 29 votes to one person! This is a recipe for disaster, especially when races are tight. And if there's a tie? The House votes, with each state getting only one vote. All this political theater just shows how broken the Electoral College is. A simple popular vote count would make things clear in days.

The Electoral College messes with how much each vote matters. Because of the winner-take-all thing, swing states become like magnets for campaigns while everyone else gets ignored. Wouldn't it be way better to just count everyone's vote across the nation? Dumping the Electoral College would simplify elections and chill things out, reducing all the drama.

Basically, the Electoral College just feels unfair and pointless. The reasons for keeping it are shaky, and the arguments against a popular vote are even shakier. Let's be real: the Electoral College is a source of needless chaos. Switching to a popular vote would make presidential elections way smoother and might actually help people get along better.

Fellow State Senators, I urge you to consider this crucial change: let's move away from the Electoral College and choose presidents based on who gets the most votes overall. After diving deep into the Electoral College – how it really works and what people think about it – I'm convinced that sticking with it just isn't the right thing to do. Picking the president by popular vote? That's clearly the better way to go.

The Electoral College is a mess, right? Posner makes a great point: it's not a true democracy. He explains that people don't directly vote for the president. Instead, "electors vote for the president, not the people…you're voting for a slate of electors." So, your vote goes to people who have already decided who they'll vote for. The Office of the Federal Register says it simply: "The Electoral College is a process, not a place." It's all about the steps you take.

Plumer brings up a scary thought: "Maybe the scariest thing is if the electoral vote ends in a tie." If that happens, the House of Representatives decides. But get this – "Because each state casts only one vote, the single representative from Wyoming, representing 500,000 voters, would have as much say as the 55 representatives from California, who represent 35 million voters." A small state has the same power as a huge state? That's not fair at all! The 2000 election, where Gore won the popular vote but Bush won the Electoral College, shows how messed up this is. Plumer sums it up well: "...the electoral college is unfair to voters...swing states..." Candidates only care about those swing states. Plumer is straightforward: "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational," because of potential ties, unfair representation, and basic flaws.

Some defend the Electoral College, saying it brings "...Certainty of Outcome...Everyone's President...Swing States...Big States...Avoid RunOff Elections..." (Posner), but these reasons don't hold up. The 2000 election was anything but certain. "Everyone's President" is wrong because electors vote, not us. Swing states get all the attention, which is unfair to everyone else. Posner thinks it helps bigger states: "The Electoral College restores some of the weight in the political balance that large states by population lose by virtue of the malapportionment of the Senate decreed in the Constitution..." But this doesn't fix the underlying unfairness. Posner also claims it "avoids that problem of elections in which no candidate receives a majority of the votes cast," but the swing state focus makes the vote unequal.

To wrap things up, we need to switch to the popular vote. The current system is broken, just like we saw in the 2000 election. Swing states have too much power, and the threat of ties is real. It's just unfair, illogical, and skewed. Despite what some people say, a fair, democratic popular vote is the only way to go.

It's super common for Americans to own cars, and many think they absolutely need them for transportation. However, there are alternative ways to travel, and an increasing number of individuals are making an effort to cut back on driving. Sure, it can be challenging at times, but the potential benefits are substantial.

Automobiles significantly pollute the air, negatively impacting both our health and the environment. Take Paris, for instance. They implemented driving restrictions to combat air pollution. According to Source 2, after five days of smog, traffic volume decreased by an impressive 60%! Just one day of reduced traffic made a noticeable difference. Think about the potential improvements if we reduced driving for weeks or even months. We don't necessarily have to eliminate cars altogether, but it's wise to consider driving less.

Interestingly, reducing driving can actually reduce stress and improve well-being. Alternative transportation methods can be more relaxing. In Vauban, Germany, parking spaces, driveways, and garages were removed, which prompted people to walk to nearby stores. Heidrun Walter, a media coach and mother, mentioned in Source 1 that she felt significantly less stressed after giving up her car. If a busy mother can manage without a car, many others can too. Being indoors with family all the time can be hard. Making walking easier and cheaper than parking encourages people to go outside, socialize, and enjoy the fresh air. It offers a refreshing change from being confined in a car.

Ultimately, minimizing driving is beneficial. I fully support it! But to encourage more people to drive less, we need to improve access to locations typically reached by car.

Automobiles have been a common transportation method for an extended period. Nevertheless, they have generated several concerns. Could we reduce our car use? Doing so might boost activity levels, purify the air, and yield significant cost savings for individuals and municipalities.

An important advantage of minimizing car usage is the possibility of increased physical activity. Indeed, automobiles have contributed to sedentary lifestyles, supplanting walking, biking, and even skating. A prime illustration is Vauban, Germany. With limited car ownership, children play and bikes are common. This shows fewer automobiles translate to greater activity. Bogota, Columbia's "Day Without Cars," was successful, and the city developed over 118 miles of bike lanes in just three years. Plainly, reduced driving improves personal health.

Furthermore, decreasing car reliance would significantly benefit the environment, as cars are major pollution sources. Notably, European cars generate roughly 12 percent of greenhouse gases, while U.S. cars produce almost 50 percent! This contaminates the air, particularly in urban centers. Apart from power plants, cars are among the worst offenders. Therefore, minimized driving promotes a cleaner world, because cars pollute significantly, and reducing their use is environmentally beneficial.

Ultimately, reduced driving can yield considerable financial benefits for both drivers and cities. Maintaining a car is expensive, with fuel, repairs, and other costs. In April 2013, driving decreased by 9 percent, reaching its lowest point since 1995! This change was driven by unaffordability of new cars for numerous Americans. Car ownership has become a substantial expense. Cities also invest significantly in transportation, with highways receiving the majority (80%) and other methods receiving less (20%) -- something public officials find unfair. Clearly, decreased driving facilitates cost savings.

Envision a world sans automobiles; picture entirely vacant roads. No indications of congestion, solely serenity! A multitude would perceive that as a superior existence. Verily, diminished driving would substantially aid our globe. A decrease in those fuel-guzzling, swift vehicles could yield wonders for the Earth. Furthermore, we'd conserve funds and enhance overall safety.

Commendably, automotive enterprises are endeavoring to fabricate hybrid and eco-conscious vehicles. Nonetheless, the paramount action we can undertake is simply possessing fewer automobiles in toto. Research indicates that greenhouse emissions could plummet "...up to 50 percent in certain car-laden locales within the United States." Additionally, there exist other noxious elements – carbon compounds, unpleasant smog. Lo and behold, Paris was compelled to prohibit driving owing to smog! "...Last week Paris registered 147 micrograms of PM per cubic meter contrasted with 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London...". These figures are escalating alarmingly in Europe and the US. Imagine the potential severity in two decades if circumstances persist along this trajectory! Fewer automobiles can mitigate these levels. It can impede these emissions from disrupting the environment and purify the sullied atmosphere.

Who disdains fiscal savings? Contemplate conserving thousands annually! By curtailing driving, you can accrue substantial savings by availing yourself of the bus, eschewing car insurance premiums, or even forgoing automobile ownership entirely. Certain individuals deem the absence of an automobile inconvenient. Undeniably, they necessitate a means of conveyance. However, in Vauban, Germany, automobiles are scarce, yet residents thrive. Reportedly, "Vauban, housing 5,500 inhabitants within a rectangular square mile, may constitute the most sophisticated experiment in lowcar suburban existence". They traverse on foot, by bicycle, or via tram. Presently, numerous American metropolises proffer rideshare applications, bus routes, and van services. Instead of squandering resources on gasoline, car installments, and insurance, attempt ridesharing or engaging in physical activity!

Upon witnessing someone drive away, do you ever harbor apprehension? Statistics demonstrate that automobile collisions engender considerably more fatalities and traumas than alternative modes of locomotion. A dearth of automobiles would correlate with diminished intoxicated driving, fewer mishaps, and a reduction in individuals sustaining injuries or fatalities. We invariably hear accounts of adolescents driving under the influence and precipitating ghastly collisions. By utilizing buses and trains, we can avert such occurrences and safeguard our younger cohort. Furthermore, intense congestion can induce stress and irritability, potentially culminating in more accidents stemming from imprudent driving. Walking instead of driving presents a win-win scenario – invigorating air and exercise!

Thus, automobiles possess both merits and demerits. They facilitate ease of transit, yet they degrade the environment, incur substantial expenses, and are not consistently secure. By diminishing our reliance on driving, we can bolster the environment by curtailing greenhouse emissions, pollution, and carbon-based constituents. Additionally, we can amass considerable financial savings by circumventing automobile-related expenditures. And a reduction in automobiles translates to fewer instances of intoxicated driving and collisions, consequently yielding fewer deaths and injuries. The subsequent time you traverse on foot, merely contemplate momentarily the potential nature of existence devoid of automobiles.

Across the globe, places are actively working to decrease car use. We see this through car-free zones, restrictions on when people can drive, and even special car-free days. A big reason for this is pollution, especially from old, smoky diesel trucks. Some places, like Vauban in Germany, plus Paris and Bogotá, are trying out interesting ideas. And guess what? They're seeing real success, which gives us hope.

Here's something cool: In Vauban, Germany, most folks don't own cars at all. There's no street parking, and the "new zone" isn't car-friendly. You can park far away, on the outskirts, but parking is super limited. If you want a spot, it is a $40,000 garage outside of the area!

But the coolest thing? Residents love not having cars. They like hearing "bikes and kids" instead of noisy cars speeding past. Vauban is a smart idea put into practice. Back in 2006, many new residents actually sold their cars after they moved there!

Vauban is part of Germany's 20-year plan to make walking and biking in the suburbs easy, and to have good bus routes. Imagine that: around 5,500 folks in just one square mile! It's a great car-free suburb. Germany's trying hard to make car-free living simple. Instead of malls far away, by the highway, stores are located right on main streets, super close to homes.

While Germany's pushing for towns where cars aren't needed, others still prefer sprawling suburbs with garages, like you see around New York City. Some people like the old ways. But get this: Vauban's ideas are catching on!

Paris, France, banned certain cars to deal with bad air, even worse than Beijing's! The city told drivers with license plates ending in even numbers to stay home. If you broke the rule, it was a 22 euro fine (or $31). The next day, it was odd-numbered plates that were banned.

Would you believe it? Almost 4,000 cars got towed! But get this: traffic went down by about 60%! French officials blamed diesel cars, even though diesel fuel got a tax break. Guess what? Most cars in France run on diesel.

Paris really struggles with air quality due to diesel cars. This ban made life hard for some, like delivery trucks. But there were good points! Electric cars, hybrids, and those with 3+ passengers could still drive. Plus, buses and trains were free. The air got much better, and the ban was lifted after only a few days.

Bogotá, Colombia, started "Car-Free Day," hoping other cities might copy them. Millions walked, skated, biked, or hopped on the bus. Colombia has done this every year, for three years now. Driving without a permit costs $25. The city, with seven million residents, is aiming for less smog, hoping to encourage different ways to travel.

Even though it rained, more folks showed up than expected! Two more cities in Colombia joined the effort for the first time! Leaders from other countries visited Bogotá to take notes. They were really interested, noting that Colombians were changing, and the concept would spread everywhere!

It all began in the mid-1990s. Bogotá built 118 miles of bike paths – the most in Latin America. They also built parks and sports areas. Small sidewalks became big walkways. Car-Free Day led to new stores and restaurants.

Studies show Americans are buying fewer cars and driving less overall. This fits with Obama's goals. The US buys tons of cars, but pollution might be turning people off.

One reason could be the recession. Many Americans couldn't afford new cars, and that's still kind of the case. This is creating problems for the big car companies, who are trying to stay in business.

Ford and Mercedes are switching things up, calling themselves "mobility companies." They now make different kinds of things. Younger people (16 to 39) are getting licenses less often. They are sharing rides, walking, or using public transportation. Many just don't see the point of having a license.

Many countries sometimes ban driving to help stop pollution. The result? Cleaner air, obviously. These countries are attacking big issues that cause even bigger ones, like global warming. Things are changing, and that's a good thing for all of us.

Senator, even with all due respect, the Electoral College is still, in my view, the best way to pick our leaders! It has a couple of pluses. Like, swing states become way more important. And, the vote count kinda matches the size of each state's population. Last, it stops someone from winning just 'cause they're super popular in one tiny area.

Swing states are like, total battle zones because of the Electoral College. This really matters, and it makes people think harder. Folks in these states know their votes mean a lot, so they're way more likely to actually choose. This makes it more likely the best person wins! If we just used the popular vote, swing states wouldn't matter nearly as much without the whole winner-take-all thing.

Also, how electoral votes are handed out, based on how many people live in each state, is super important. People who want to be president go after bigger states 'cause they have more electoral votes. But if we used the popular vote, they could just skip the big states and focus on grabbing lots of little ones. The system we have now is balanced. Smaller states won't be too strong.

On top of that, the Electoral College stops anyone from winning just 'cause they're liked somewhere. Candidates gotta chase votes, even where they're not so welcome. If it was popular vote, campaigns would be a mess. It's hard to figure out where to focus without the winner-take-all setup. The Electoral College blocks candidates who are only popular in one place from winning, because there'd be no reason to spread out.

So, yeah, I think the Electoral College is way better than the popular vote for picking presidents! To sum it up, these are the main reasons why I think it's a better method.

Seriously, like, why is the Electoral College *still* here for picking presidents? Shouldn't we be over this old thing already? The U.S. needs to change; the person with the most votes *should* just win, that's it.

The winner-takes-all thing *sounds* easy, but is it really fair? The Federal Register says most states do it like this: win the state, get all the votes. Some say working hard in a state should mean you get all the votes, even if you *barely* win. That's a big risk, right? Maybe that's why they kept it. And what about article one? People chosen by their *party* vote. So, we vote for *them* so they can vote for the person? That's crazy. People should just vote! We gotta get rid of the Electoral College.

Bradford Plumer's piece, "The Indefensible Electoral College," makes a good point. We aren't voting *right* for the president; we vote for people, and *they* pick the winner. Every vote *has* to be equal! Some votes being worth more because of where you live? That's not okay! Plumer says, "If you lived in Texas... you'd vote for a group of 34 Democratic electors for Kerry.” Why should Kerry get 34 votes just because his party picked them? He should only get votes from people who *picked* him! If they don't like him, their vote shouldn't matter. All votes need to be equal; get rid of it now.

Plumer brings up something scary: what if it's a tie? What happens then? The House decides, with each *state* getting one vote. That doesn't seem right at all! Wyoming, a *tiny* state, has the same power as California, with *millions* of people? That's totally unfair! Popular votes *hardly* ever lead to ties. Like Plumer says, "Al Gore... won the popular vote but lost the presidency." The people didn't win because of the *Electoral College*! Bush won instead. It's broken and makes more problems.

We need a popular vote now. It’s better for everyone and way fairer for people running. The Electoral College needs to go, forever.

Alright, let's face it: cars make life a breeze! But do we ever stop and think about the downside of always being behind the wheel? We're gonna dig into what happens when we're too car-dependent – the messy problems and some smart ways to turn things around.

Big problem number one? Cars spewing out greenhouse gases left and right! Believe it or not, cars in Europe are responsible for around 12% of those emissions. But get this: a town in Germany, Vauban, is trying something totally different. They're going for a "no car" vibe, which could change everything. Other places are starting to dig this idea, picturing cool, compact neighborhoods with awesome public transport. As Heidrun Walter, a resident, put it, "With a car, I was always stressed. I have a happier life now." Sure, it's a big shift, but some people are finding life gets way better.

And then there's the whole smog thing. Paris got so choked with smog that the government had to stop folks from driving just to clear the air! Plus, cheap diesel in France just made the smog party even worse. It's a wake-up call that the rules need to change! Good news is, stats show that fewer Americans are buying cars, getting their licenses, and actually driving. Since America can be a big polluter, this could really help. And young people connecting online instead of hitting the road? That's a win too! In the words of Bill Ford from Ford Motor Company: "Pedestrians, bicycles, cars, and public transport can work together in a connected system to save time, use less stuff, make less pollution, and be safer."

To fight smog, Bogota, Colombia, is pushing people to find other ways to get around. Carlos Arturo, a businessman from the area, points out, "It's a good chance to get rid of stress and make the air cleaner." The city is throwing money into parks, better sidewalks, charging fees for driving in busy spots, and sprucing up the shops. And guess what? It's working!

So, there you have it: a few cool examples of what happens when we're not so obsessed with cars. Judging by these early signs, it's pretty clear that even bigger, better changes are possible as more people give the car-free life a shot.

So, when folks go to pick the President, they pick their fave. But hold up—sometimes, even if your fave gets the most votes, they can still lose! We have this thing called the Electoral College. Basically, you win a state, you snag all its "electoral votes." Then, these "electors" actually cast the votes. I think it stinks, and lots of others do too. Messes up who gets a voice, makes people not wanna vote, and pushes candidates to only care about a few states.

The main trouble with the Electoral College? It's just not fair. States with tons of people get more "electoral votes." Wyoming, for example, has around 500,000 voters and doesn't get many votes. This makes each person's vote worth less when you're trying to get 270. Seriously, it just feels wrong. Plus, the person who got the most votes overall can still lose – like Bush versus Gore in 2000, anyone remember?

Some argue that giving electoral votes based on how many people live in a state is okay. But, a national popular vote would be much fairer. Whoever gets the most votes across the whole country wins, period. In states where one party dominates, like Republicans in Texas, that party always wins. A Texas Democrat might feel like their vote doesn't matter, so they just don't bother voting. That's bad for voters and a smack in the face to what America is supposed to be.

When a state's population grows, its Electoral College power goes up. So, candidates really focus on these "big states," and talk to their voters. States with fewer people? They often get ignored. Candidates travel, give speeches, but totally skip some states. Back in 2000, seventeen states were overlooked! This makes many in those states lose interest in voting.

The Electoral College is a mess. It turns off voters, makes fewer people vote, and makes candidates only care about big states. Switching to a national popular vote would fix these issues and really make presidential elections better.

Esteemed Senator,

I am contacting you to vocalize my worries about the Electoral College (EC), considering both the good and bad aspects. Although this system picks the president and vice president, many question whether it's truly fair. Some say it's old-fashioned and unfair. They'd rather have a simple popular vote. While the Electoral College might seem annoying, since we don't directly pick the president, it does have some upsides. It fixes issues and makes voting simpler. It also makes the number of votes needed smaller to get a sure win. It's meant to ensure everyone in the U.S. can share what they think. But is it worth all this? Does simplicity make it okay to keep the public from directly picking the president? Certain folks think the Electoral College is a helpful "extra" to ensure voting goes smoothly. But, lots of Americans don't agree. They believe it hurts the U.S. It's quite clear the Electoral College is in huge need of an update.

The EC idea as a "just in case" for voting makes many worried. Even though this idea sounds good, it often makes representation harder. People vote for party electors, who should be supporting the person their party nominated. Richard Posner, in his work, mentions the Electoral College winner might not always get the most votes from the public. He says this doesn't happen too often. Posner is right, it's rare. The EC mostly works where the candidate with the most votes in a state gets all the electoral votes from that state. This can cause problems. A president should be the one representing the majority, but there are going to be upset voters. The winner should get the most votes to truly show what Americans want. Educated voters learn about candidates and then decide who's best. Voting lets people choose who should be president. When the most people pick someone, they ought to win. However, the EC gives all state electoral votes to the top candidate in the state. Because of this, some people who a popular vote would represent fairly might not actually get represented. This is bad for the nation, including the president.

A big issue with the Electoral College is that it stops Americans from directly voting for their president. Those who like it say "the EC avoids problems with polling everyone in America." They say polling everyone brings up problems, like too many votes, which is hard to count. However, making sure votes are right is the president's job. They have to represent the people who voted for them by letting them pick the president. Here, "easy" is their excuse to stop voting. A popular vote could have mistakes, but the Electoral College has fewer electors, so there are fewer mistakes. The EC tries to look like the population, ensuring the candidate will work for an area, and not just one place. Still, the EC isn't perfect. Posner states, "the smartest voters should decide the election," and he's totally right. An uneducated voter is a danger to any nation. Yet, the US promises every citizen the right to vote. No one in charge can just say "only the educated should decide," as figuring out who is an educated voter is tough.

The EC's issues are made worse by big states having power. More people means a popular vote winner represents those people, no matter where. Smaller states say they need a say in the Senate and EC. Each state got two senators, no matter how big, thanks to the Senate. However, the Electoral College does not give smaller states an equal say. A candidate winning a popular vote relies on the state's population. The EC also relies on the state's population. Because smaller states don't have many electoral votes, they want equal votes in the US Senate. Sadly, the EC doesn't do that.

Understanding where this comes from helps make real change. Always aim for accuracy, no matter how difficult. Keeping the EC lacks accuracy and only cares about ease. A good Electoral College should support popular votes. I'm not happy with the current EC. Some things can't be changed, but trying for the best way is important. Senator, we voted for you. We trust you to defend our best interests. We want you to represent us when picking a president. Because of this, we want the Electoral College gone to make a better voting system.

Alright, so, cars – they’re good for long hauls, definitely. But hey, we’ve got buses, bikes, all kinds of stuff for getting around town. Seriously, ditching the car a bit is a win. Yeah, cars are easy, I know, but there's cheaper stuff out there!

Listen up – trains and buses haul people, solo or the whole crew. These save juice, fight that nasty pollution, and make getting around safer. Cars smash up when folks are sleepy, or can’t nail that parking spot. And, duh, cars bleed your wallet dry! Gas, repairs – it never quits. Strapped for cash? Subways, buses, way cheaper. And trust me, getting towed? Nightmare fuel. When the air’s still, exhaust just hangs there, upping the yuck factor. People whine they need a car, but public transit proves them wrong, right?

Plus, driving less helps our planet breathe. Roads jammed? Less driving means less of that gross smog. Car-free ideas? They show how sweet things could be with way less driving – chill vibes, clean air. That’s huge! We could get more green spaces, walks that don’t suck, less traffic jams, cool shops popping up. Even if the U.S. loves its cars, new problems might stop the non-stop driving, especially when you clock all the awesome stuff that could happen!

So, yeah, cars, they matter, they’re lifesavers sometimes. But – hear me out – we can totally live without them now and then. Sure, cars are key, I get it, but what about Mother Earth? Cars are easy, no doubt, but loads of cheap ways to get around exist.

That never-ending noise? Alarms, horns, engines screaming. It's just life now, the background to our days. Honestly, how many cars there are is crazy!

We have to drive less. Seriously, it matters a lot.

Think of all the good things that could happen!

Cars feel needed, but are they? Long trips need them, sure. But a quick trip nearby? Not needed at all!

Driving less is super important. Families spend so much on gas each week – like $20 to $50! Drive less, save more cash. You'll have money for important things and fun times.

“Having a car felt hard. Life's better without it," someone said. People who get rid of their cars? They seem way happier.

Plus, the air gets better! Everyone gets healthier!

“It helps with stress and cleans the air," says Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman biking with his wife. People who barely drive? They always love it.

If everyone knew how good driving less is and did it, the air would be fresher, and everyone would feel better.

One person who used to drive said her kids "...plan their summers around walking, buses, or sharing rides.” And they don't even care about getting their licenses, even though cars help sometimes. Some would rather walk than drive!

Driving isn't bad, but less driving? It's really great. You save cash walking, the air gets cleaner for everyone, and you focus on what's important: work, friends, and family.

Try it out! Put your car away! Walking is a nice change.

Senator, please listen to me, just a student, about getting rid of the Electoral College – and hear why! We really need to pick the president by who gets the most votes from everyone in the country! Think about it: every single vote would count. Not this weird system we have now.

Seriously, doesn't our way of doing things kind of pick the president in a roundabout way? These electors, picked by state parties, take away our say. We really need to have more control over who leads us.

Remember that time in the '60s when Louisiana almost messed up picking their electors? Crazy! That close call showed us how messed up the Electoral College could be. It almost caused total chaos! Keeping this system? We're just asking for trouble later.

See: How the "winner-take-all" thing? It messes up campaigns too. Candidates only care about states that could go either way. What happens to voters in states that are pretty much decided already? They get ignored!

Honestly, the Electoral College? It's old and broken. It stops us from directly picking our leader, causes problems, and makes elections unfair with its silly "winner-take-all" rule. We deserve a clear system that avoids past mistakes and fixes that ridiculous "winner-take-all" issue.

The Electoral College system, featuring electors acting as intermediaries in presidential elections, undoubtedly presents notable issues. Our votes don't directly elect the president. Rather, we designate individuals to make the selection for us, somewhat obscuring the true significance of our votes. Wouldn't a simple aggregation of all individual votes offer a more logical approach? To be frank, the electoral college exhibits some undemocratic qualities.

While ostensibly designed to benefit smaller states, larger states still wield considerable influence. Certain states possess substantial electoral vote allocations, whereas others have very few. However, if small states were to coordinate their efforts, they could collectively exert more influence than larger ones. Moreover, a candidate could secure the most votes overall but ultimately lose the election if their supporters are primarily concentrated in specific areas. The Electoral College has a tendency to skew popular vote outcomes.

Reconciling discrepancies between the popular vote and electoral vote outcomes can be perplexing. A candidate might triumph in California, amassing a significant number of electoral votes. A comparable performance in Montana, however, would yield substantially fewer votes. Despite comparable campaign efforts in both locations, the electoral vote system favors candidates who perform exceptionally well in populous states.

Adding to the complexity, the Electoral College operates on a "winner-takes-all" basis. Candidates tend to concentrate their campaign efforts in states such as California and New York. Even a narrow victory in these states secures all of their electoral votes. Even in a close race, such as a 49% to 51% split, the losing candidate receives nothing. A fairer system would proportionally allocate electoral votes based on the actual vote count for each candidate.

In essence, the Electoral College is far from ideal. A simple aggregation of the popular vote would offer a more transparent, equitable, and easily comprehensible approach, devoid of convoluted explanations.

Okay, so things are pretty tough right now, right? The planet's in trouble, and lots of folks are struggling to pay bills. We need a fix, like, yesterday! Here's an idea: Drive less! It hits two big problems. Less cars mean better air, which is good for the Earth. Plus, people can save a ton of cash, which really helps when money's tight. Basically, driving less is a win-win!

The planet is really getting trashed by pollution. Robert Duffer points out that some days, Paris can have air just as nasty as Beijing! So, the French government got serious, made tough rules about driving, and fined people who broke them. And guess what? It cut down on smog! But then they dropped the rules. Still, big lesson here: In places with bad air, these kinds of rules can really clean things up and make people healthier.

If you're broke, ditching your car can be a lifesaver. All that saved money can go to stuff you actually need. Elisabeth Rosenthal talks about German towns where, get this, 70% of people don't even own a car! Why? Parking's a nightmare and costs a fortune – like, up to $40,000! Giving up a car is way cheaper. Some folks even sold their cars and moved there. Rosenthal says going car-free can save you big bucks, especially if you're strapped for cash.

So, yeah, there are tons of good reasons to drive less. Cleaner air means a healthier world for everyone. And you save money, which is super important if you're struggling. Some people might complain that it makes getting to work or school harder, but there are other ways to get around! Think about what happens when we drive too much: more pollution and wasting money on gas and car repairs. By driving less, we make those problems smaller.

Senator, hear me out!

We must abolish the Electoral College, right now! Sure, it's historical, dating back to the Constitution, but it's time to retire it. Each individual vote must count, no question. The Electoral College? Simply unjust. Furthermore, certain states are unfairly disadvantaged! We demand genuine freedom and equality, and this system obstructs progress.

The U.S. constantly boasts universal rights, right? Yet, is that truth if citizens can't directly elect the president? You elect electors, who then select the president. Seriously, what's the point of casting votes? It's a major concern when electors defy the popular will. Bradford Plumer noted Al Gore "won the vote but lost the presidency" in 2000 due to this. And listen: a Gallup poll revealed over 60 percent of voters favor a simple popular vote. Also, in 1960, there was an attempt to switch electors in Louisiana against John F. Kennedy. Election manipulation! It's infrequent, but what if it becomes commonplace? Americans must select their leaders, completely.

The Electoral College creates significant inequality in the U.S. because states are treated inconsistently. The winner-takes-all approach means candidates only prioritize specific states – large ones, swing states, and so on. Bradford Plumer pointed out that "during the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't even see the candidates." How messed up is that? How can people make informed choices if candidates neglect them? Equality entails a fair vote for everyone, with no exceptions.

We must eliminate the Electoral College, as it's unfair to both individuals and states. Abolishing it will allow the U.S. to honor its commitments.

Sincerely,

A U.S. Citizen.

We shape our routines with how the world works, and despite the issue of pollution, our efforts to combat it are on the rise. Numerous nations are contributing to this fight, including the U.S., Colombia, France, and Germany. Interestingly, certain individuals in Germany find joy without cars! Once a year, Colombia observes a car-free day, which brings a welcome respite from the pressures and contamination of everyday life; disobey the rules, and face the consequences. Might limiting our reliance on cars enhance our quality of life?

Vauban, a German town aiming for car-free status by 2006, saw incredible results! Approximately 70% of households are without vehicles, and over half of the residents had sold their cars prior to relocating there. Cars are seldom seen; there is an absence of street parking, garages, and driveways. A tram efficiently traverses the town along the main thoroughfare. Similar initiatives to Vauban are emerging across Europe, symbolizing a substantial shift. The EPA is promoting car-lite zones in the U.S., and several politicians support this initiative, describing it as "smart planning," which includes compact towns, convenient walking routes, and a reduced reliance on driving to stores.

Paris was overwhelmed by significant pollution issues, leading to the enactment of a law restricting driving on specific days in an effort to improve air quality. One Monday, cars bearing odd-numbered license plates were prohibited from being driven. Those who defied the law were promptly fined! The following day, a ban was placed on even-numbered plates. Nearly 4,000 individuals were penalized, and 27 vehicles were towed, creating a stir among some residents. Nevertheless, despite the turbulence, car traffic experienced a 30% decrease!

Bogota, Colombia, launched a car-free day that has evolved into a significant occurrence. An increase in walking and cycling leads to fresher air. Driving is restricted to buses and taxis exclusively. Millions can be seen engaging in skating, biking, walking, or hiking, regardless of the weather. Many people note that it alleviates stress and allows them to breathe cleaner air. Valledupar and Cali, also located in Colombia, are carrying out similar initiatives. The mayor of Asuncion, Paraguay, even visited to witness this movement. The city has introduced cycling paths, improved sidewalks, athletic spaces, and parks to improve the pedestrian experience.

Do you now see why many nations are dedicated to safeguarding our planet? Car-free days and zones have a considerable positive effect. I trust that I have inspired you to consider embracing a lifestyle with more living and less driving.

Senator, this Electoral College? It bugs me. Its age? So weird. I want it gone! It hurts voters, could cause a tie, and – crazy thing – we don't even vote directly for the president!

The College? So unfair. Candidates just care about swing states. These states give ALL votes to the winner, so others? They're ignored. Like they don't matter! In 2000, like, seventeen states got NO attention. Media? No ads. Those voters got so little info! Candidates hide stuff! No ads, no visits, so understanding choices? Hard! College unfair.

What if the College ties? Ugh, disaster. House decides, each state, one vote. Seriously? Outcome might not match state wants. Depends on few voters, so bad. A tie kills the election's vibe.

Also, our presidential link? Broken. We vote electors, not president. So, a Texan liking John Kerry? Backing 34 Dems who say they'll vote for him. And anyone can be an elector. Even someone not in politics might be biased. The College? Confusing.

Senator, the College? Bad. Voters sad, ties possible, election not direct. Voters feel wrong. A tie? Horrible. Electors, not president! It’s just wrong, old, and confusing.

Thinking of ditching the car? Awesome! Doing so offers real perks: you shrink your carbon footprint, keep more cash in your pocket, and ditch those awful traffic snarls.

One HUGE benefit? You're doing the planet a solid. Elizabeth Rosenthal points out cars cough up a TON of greenhouse gasses. In Europe, we're talking around 12%. But here in the US, land of the automobile, that number can skyrocket to 50%! All that exhaust cooks up smog, particularly in urban jungles. Robert Duffer chats about Paris' efforts to boot cars and curb smog. Some even dream of car-free suburbs. Bottom line: fewer cars, cleaner air.

Fewer cars also equals lighter traffic! Think of Bogota, Colombia. They had a "no car day" to clear the air and grease the wheels of traffic. Less congestion translates to less stress, and people zipping around like clockwork. Say goodbye to tardiness! Folks hoofed it, hopped on bikes, or rode the bus. Bogota even threw in parks and sports fields. Bottom line: fewer cars = healthier humans and a healthier planet.

Finally, there are financial gains! You're buying less gas, and you can kiss those car expenses goodbye. Your tax bill shrinks for roads, parking, and lighting. Less money wasted on roads and parking garages. City dwellers already embrace subways and buses to keep their wallets happy. Less car use is an easy way to dial down pollution.

All in all, driving less is a win-win. It's great for the Earth, eases traffic, gets you moving, and pads your bank account. Sure, it's tougher if you're in the boonies, but it's a slam dunk in the city!

So, cars are everywhere, right? Globally, it's difficult to miss them! People need them to visit family, travel for work, and move about and stuff. However, living without a car? This is becoming a thing in spots in Europe and America. Take Vauban, a German town; cars are practically banned. Heidrun Walter, a mom there, said, "I was stressed with a car; now I'm happier." It seems like it can work and make you feel great, right?

Yes, it's difficult, but there are major payoffs. Cars make up about 12% of Europe's greenhouse gasses, and half in the U.S. according to reports! Driving less cuts down on pollution – huge. Remember Paris? They stopped cars because of smog, only letting some in on specific days. Traffic went down 60%, with cleaner air! With shifts like that, what if all cities were car-free? The upsides are massive!

Going car-free isn't the only option. Have you heard of Bogota, Colombia? They do a car-free day every year; it's popular. For three years, they nixed cars (besides buses and taxis) so people could try other travel methods and curb smog in their city of seven million. Breaking the rules? They fine you! Also, Cali and Valledupar joined, too! Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, said, "It's great to clean the air." A short time can make a difference.

Check this out: America might be ditching cars, reports say. Americans aren't buying vehicles, driving as much, and rates for licenses dipped. Doug Short from Advisor Perspectives found we drove the most in 2005, and it's trended down since then. By 2013, driving fell almost 9% from the peak. As a result, Americans need fewer vehicles. This cuts down carbon emissions and helps the environment. Other travel options mean things shift. NYC has bike sharing, and San Francisco has BART. So many choices exist.

In conclusion, ditching the car and other travel options are great! Green travel is easier when you drive less. The choices are out there, and the good outweighs the bad.

Senator Adams, get this Presidential election headache! Instead of voters picking the prez directly, the Electoral College decides. Political parties choose electors, yet they sometimes disregard directions. Even if the most people vote for someone, they don't automatically win. It confuses voters, and they get mad about it. This old system should change!

These electors cause worries! Parties pick them, and state rules are constantly changing. The regular people have little say in who is picked. Counting individual votes would be a lot simpler. We require certainty to make sure the people's wishes are considered. The electors currently have too much power!

Imagine winning the overall vote, but losing because of not hitting a certain number of electors. The disappointment is real, as electors decide everything. Gore had more votes than Bush but still lost, right? Similar issues can happen! Even if it is close, someone could still win without the popular vote. Definitely not fair.

Nobody actually understands the Electoral College! It messes up the people's vote, stated in one piece. The existing system doesn't mirror the overall vote. One person remembered voting for electors, but wasn't even certain on who they were. People are left bewildered!

We ought to ditch the Electoral College, and choose the popular vote. Because electors are party loyalists, they may not be trustworthy. There's a chance to win the overall vote and still lose because of electors. A major improvement is needed. It is all very confusing for the people.

Listen up, Senator!

So, the Electoral College, yeah? Still causing problems. Wouldn't it be better to just get rid of it? See, it's confusing. People pick electors, and *then* those electors pick the president. Here's the crazy part: those electors can change their minds! Seriously! They can vote for whoever they want. Just imagine one vote flipping the whole election! People want something different. Remember 2000, when Gore won the popular vote but lost? Polls back then showed most people wanted a direct vote.

Okay, Bush v. Kerry isn't common, but it *did* happen. Do we really want that to happen again? We believe in democracy, right? Power to the people? But *do* we really have that power? It feels like it, but the Electoral College – just 538 people – actually decides. Seriously, it's a bad system! It's easily manipulated and can favor one party. Back in 1960, there were shady attempts to remove Democratic electors in Louisiana to block Kennedy. Scary, huh?

And electoral votes? Totally unfair! Look at the House. Bigger states get more votes. California? 55 votes! Huge compared to small states like South Dakota or Alaska. The Senate helps balance things in Congress, but that doesn't happen here. So the big states win again! And if there's a tie? The House decides, but each state gets *one* vote. How crazy is that?

So, to sum it up, the Electoral College is old, unfair, and needs to be fixed. We could make it easy by letting everyone vote directly for the president. No electors! Honestly, the current system isn't truly democratic and needs to go.

Thanks!

Okay, the Electoral College? *Strange*, right? Think back to when America began. The founders put it in the Constitution! They didn't want Congress to choose *everything*. Nor did they want *everyone* voting immediately. *Electors* vote, and then Congress counts it up. Seems so old fashioned, no? Does it matter *now*? Does what you vote even count? Some think electors are smart and careful. But lots of folks feel like they don't have a big voice, do they?

It seems simple to vote. But you're not picking the President, are you? You're choosing *electors*! *Then* they pick the President. Even if most people vote another way! Think of them as the big vote! You might win the most votes, but still lose. Because of the electors! This has happened before, hasn't it? Remember 2000? Gore won the vote, but lost the whole thing because of the college. Some say leave it alone. But what if we changed it?

Remember, the Electoral College is *really old*! But, it can mess things up a lot! The actual choice can be ignored, you know? Maybe a *new* way is better. Perhaps a way with everyone voting, not just electors!

The Electoral College is old and broken, get it? It needs work. And changes. Not to be left alone! Your vote feels small, doesn't it? *Fix* the college! Otherwise, what's the point?

Dear Senator, I've been thinking about presidential elections. The Electoral College confuses me. I get why it existed back when the country was new. But now that we know about the "538 electors," is it still the best method? A simple popular vote seems easier, right? The Office of the Federal Register says, "The Electoral College consists of the selection of the electors," and to win, "a majority of 270 electoral votes is required." Does our voice even count? Surely there's a better way!

This is a huge issue, but you can solve it! Since "electors are generally chosen by the candidate's political party," shouldn't everyday people have more say? Also, "the presidential election is held every four years," so people wait a while to be heard. Why not a direct vote? That's what they want! Currently, "most states have a winner-takes-all system that awards all electors to the winning presidential candidate. However, Maine and Nebraska each have a variation of proportional representation." Why not skip the fuss and let people directly vote for President? Isn't that truly American?

"According to a Gallup poll in 2000, taken shortly after Al Gore won the popular vote but lost the presidency, over 60% of voters would prefer a direct election." Plus, Bradford Plumer said, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational. The best arguments in favor of it are mostly assertions without much basis in reality. And the arguments against direct elections are spurious at best."

As a Florida resident, I favor the Electoral College; it just *feels* right to me, you know? It seems to boost the odds we'll get a president most of us actually dig. It streamlines elections, pulls us together, and hey, it's kinda a big deal in those swing states.

Now, sure, the Electoral College ain't perfect, but ditching it for a straight-up popular vote? Might not be so great! It often blows a win up bigger than just the raw vote count. Plus, it throws a bone to the smaller states, which is cool. And get this, even with all those 538 electors, ties hardly ever happen. The winner has to play fair with everyone. No picking favorites based on what you believe, where you live, or what your mailman delivers to. Otherwise, people feel left out, and the president looks like they just don't care.

Presidential hopefuls, they laser-focus on those swing states. Ever wonder why? Some folks figure those states have the "smartest" voters, so *they* should be the ones calling the shots, capiche?

Okay, some folks grumble that the Electoral College hands too much power to the government. But hold on! Without it, that's EVEN MORE likely! The Electoral College? It actually gives the power back to US, the voters, so we can choose who gets to be president.

Bottom line? The Electoral College lets ordinary Joes and Janes thoughtfully decide who's gonna be running the show for the next four years.

The Electoral College: an enduring method for picking presidents; I really think it's essential. Its benefit? It spreads power across all states, helps ensure a clear victor, and keeps elections precisely on schedule. The folks it has chosen throughout time tend to be great at understanding what the public wants. Plus, our opinions are heard, and the Electoral College makes it all flow without hiccups.

Consider this: Large states such as Florida wield a significant number of electoral votes, up to 29!, but smaller ones, like Wyoming, have dramatically less, just 3. Each state's influence is determined by a similar mechanism to how senators and representatives are chosen. Each state is guaranteed a minimum of three votes due to the total number of residents, and this seems fair. This prevents larger states from overpowering smaller states. If Wyoming's population were to rise suddenly, it could get more votes, while Florida could drop some! Florida's votes perfectly show the state's diversity. The Electoral College forces voters to be more thoughtful. Imagine the consequences of a Californian victory due to its higher population without it.

Draws are certainly possible, however rare, due to the winner-take-all system in most states. Swing states hold huge power! Candidates avoid states that consistently vote against them, and instead prioritize those where outcomes remain unpredictable. They rely on their supporters, of course. When shopping, you would buy only what you need, not what you already have. Therefore, be mindful of the candidate's itinerary!

Presidential inaugurations occur every January 20th or March 4th, let's keep it that way! Sticking to deadlines is vital, a task that could be challenging without the Electoral College. It makes elections tidy and punctual, usually on the second Thursday in November. Imagine the chaos of eliminating the Electoral College and recounting every vote, which could take months in Texas, for example! We'd be without a leader, which could lead to real problems.

The Electoral College stands as a time-honored component of American democracy, and removing it would be extremely tricky. This is a tradition and we should embrace that. It decentralizes power, ensures a clear winner, and maintains the timing of our elections. It creates order, which benefits us all due to its current framework.

Ditch the car sometimes, okay? Seriously, think about all that cash you'd save, plus, you get free time and way less stress. I'm *almost* fifteen; honestly, that learner's permit can totally chill. Right now, a car? Nah, I don't actually need one. Everything's super close – like, food, the mall – so why bother driving at all? Think about it this way: fewer crashes, tons of money saved on gas, all just from easing off the gas a little!

Remember that nasty smog attack in Paris? Yikes, right? That made people think! Now, here's an idea: what if every country had no-car days? How amazing would that be?! Picture this: No traffic jams, good fresh air, and roads that aren't scary to use! On those days, everyone could just hop on the bus or stroll around. It would be an awesome way to clean up the air and make the streets way safer. After the war, the car ruled city planning. That has to change. Let's start putting what really matters first. Yes, cars are fast, but also, they're super polluting! Clean air is a necessity! Maybe the simple solution is to just lighten up on the driving. You know, the old rules basically dumped money on highways, like 80% of the budget versus 20% for other stuff.

Car costs? Seriously, what a racket! That cash could pay for something way more fun, like a vacation, or even your kids' education! Is drowning in car debt even worth it? Why stress out when you could actually be enjoying life? Often, cars are a pain in the wallet, causing way more headaches than help. Did you know, last year, studies showed that there were 23% fewer young drivers between 2001 and 2009! Maybe young people are finally getting that cars are overhyped. Getting your license often means boring stuff anyway, like getting groceries and chauffeuring kids around. And don't forget, gas, accidents, fines, and those annoying tolls pile up. All these car issues are pushing people to take the bus or walk!

My aunt is always griping about her car, yet she still drives to Walmart when she could easily walk! Let's try to drive less, people. Aim for cleaner air and safer streets! Way better than gross smog and those never-ending car payments. Driving less? It helps everyone!

Okay, so, the Electoral College? It’s stirred up debates about whether it should even stick around. While some defend it, many are pushing for changes. What's the core issue? Frankly, it needs a serious update, if you ask me. Right now, it feels like the system prioritizes the overall count instead of individual votes. It's strange that we don't directly pick the president and that electors could just switch things up and go their own route. Just think about a state or a single elector messing up the entire election! It makes our democracy seem unstable, doesn’t it?

Despite defenses, the Electoral College poses various challenges. Politicians often focus on states with the most electoral votes, overlooking smaller ones. For example, a candidate might spend weeks in California for its 55 votes, ignoring Montana's mere 3. Basically, it seems more about gaining electoral votes than addressing everyone's needs. It makes elections seem like they focus on a few big states, rather than representing the entire country.

Like in "The Indefensible Electoral College," it's like we pick people to pick the president, right? "Electors Election," not "Presidential Election"! Electors should follow instructions, but sometimes they don't. If an elector suddenly changed their mind, it could mess up the whole election and cause chaos for the country. Our democracy must reflect the voice of the American people, not just electors. Some say, "If it ain't broke, don't fix it," talking about the Electoral College’s long history. But shouldn't we always try to improve things by reevaluating our systems? Where would we be if we never questioned things? The Electoral College must be monitored to ensure it accurately shows who the people want as president.

Basically, the Electoral College seems more about electoral power than the American people's will. Some states have more influence, and electors have too much power. If we want a government that values and represents everyone, we must address the Electoral College. Our aim is to ensure it reflects the people's desires, so we can move forward as a country.

Alright, so what's the deal with the Electoral College? Ever stop to think where your vote *really* goes? It doesn’t pick the President directly; no chance. Instead, we vote for electors who are *meant* to vote for the President. But, seriously, can we believe they'll do what's best for all of us?

Seriously, the Electoral College just feels biased. Some votes are worth more. Okay, maybe these electors seem really smart and qualified. But can we *really* expect them to decide for us voters, who can easily decide for ourselves? It just doesn't seem right, does it? That whole winner-takes-all idea? Candidates just ignore states they think they'll lose and focus on "swing" states. Why keep a system so many people dislike? People feel like their vote is useless. The Electoral College *might* think it's so smart, but shouldn’t *we* get to pick the President? "...candidates don't bother with states they know they can't win..." Seriously though? We should pick our own leader, that's it.

Also, think about all this. How do we even know who to vote for? We *can* and *should* be careful voting for electors, and voting is super important. Make sure to vote! But we don't always know everything, right? "The American people should consider themselves lucky that the two thousand fiasco was the biggest election crisis in a century the system allows for much worse. Consider the state legislatures are technically responsible for picking electors , and that those electors could always defy the will of the people." Voters make mistakes sometimes, but not always! We should vote wisely, but the Electoral College shouldn't just ignore us. There are different opinions and different electoral parties, but hey, remember the 2000 election issue. "Consider the state legislatures are technically responsible for picking electors, and that those electors could always defy the will of the people." We have to vote for our President, but also be careful about who we're voting for, to avoid total mayhem.

So, yeah, hopefully you're considering all this now. It's hard to just give something this important to someone else. We need to believe in ourselves and make choices based on the truth.

Okay, picture this: Finally, Friday rolls around, and at 5 PM sharp, freedom rings! Forget fighting through the usual traffic jams and honking horns; it's just a smooth, easy ride home. Nice, right? Plenty of Europeans enjoy this kind of ease on a random Tuesday. This highlights how great life can be when cars don't dominate everything.

Big win: You save money! Cars are simply money-sucking machines. Boom! Accidents happen, speeding tickets arrive, and whoosh! Your wallet empties faster than you can say "insurance." Plus, when you ditch the car, you also say goodbye to gas expenses. Okay, gas might be cheap for now, but as we all know, it ALWAYS goes back up. And if the cost is too high, you might delay those necessary car repairs, which is a bad idea. But a bus? Super cheap. Driving? Even a short trip can cost you six bucks JUST for gas! But what about a bike? Bikes beat cars in terms of affordability, hands down.

Now, think about the grime: Cars blast out tons of pollution, including noise, light, and air pollution. Whether we want to admit it or not, this damages the entire planet. The U.S. might not currently be the worst, but if we add more cars, that could shift very quickly. Sure, getting rid of cars entirely might be unrealistic, but what about a little moderation? For instance, driving on specific days based on your license plate, or carpooling with colleagues? Even these small changes can significantly improve things.

Get this: Folks living in cities with fewer cars are actually happier! Less stress from traffic jams and small fender-benders. Seriously, driving can be terrible, with all those distractions. And distractions lead to accidents or simply stress you out. Heidrun Walter, a real mom, hit the nail on the head when she said: "Having a car stressed me out so much. I'm much happier now!" She loved lively streets, where bike bells and kids' laughter overpowered car sounds. Picture everything being close, so you can relax and walk or bike with your kids. Sounds pretty good, doesn’t it?

So, how might your life change with fewer cars? Could it improve your health and your overall happiness? Makes you wonder, doesn’t it? What if simply using your car less could make things significantly better?

Daily, individuals hop into their vehicles for work commutes, chores, or adventures, often overlooking the greenhouse gases damaging our world. This widespread contamination poses a major issue, especially in cityscapes.
